proposals.title: A Continuous Attractor Network Model of Primate Head Direction Coding: Linking Spatial Navigation to Scene and View Representation
proposals.motivation: The rodent head direction system is a canonical example of a continuous attractor network (CAN), crucial for spatial navigation. However, the neural basis of head direction in primates, who rely heavily on foveal vision and spatial view cells for scene representation, remains poorly understood. Modeling this system is essential to bridge Rolls' work on hippocampal spatial view cells with cortical mechanisms for spatial orientation and memory, offering a more complete computational framework for primate navigation and scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: The primate head direction system can be modeled as a continuous attractor network, but its dynamics and inputs will be fundamentally shaped by high-acuity visual scene information and spatial view representations, differing from rodent models. This integrated network will show enhanced stability during active visual exploration and facilitate the coupling of head direction to allocentric scene representations.
proposals.method: We will develop a CAN model using integrate-and-fire neurons with asymmetric recurrent connections to sustain a stable activity bump representing head direction. The model will be informed by primate neurophysiology (e.g., from the presubiculum and retrosplenial cortex). Crucially, we will incorporate visual input layers simulating the response properties of ventral visual stream and parahippocampal scene-selective regions, providing visual landmark-driven resetting. The model's dynamics will be tested under simulated primate foraging tasks using simulated eye movements to update spatial view inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational model of the primate head direction system, directly linking it to the spatial view cell framework. It will generate testable predictions for neural recordings in primates and elucidate how visual scene processing stabilizes orientation. The model will be a key component in a larger systems-level understanding of how reward, memory, and spatial navigation interact in the primate brain.
proposals.novelty: While CAN models exist for rodent head direction, applying this framework to primates requires integrating high-level visual and scene representations, a synthesis not yet attempted. This moves beyond modeling a generic directional system to one specifically shaped by the primate's visual cognitive strategy, directly connecting Rolls' theories of scene representation with canonical navigation circuits.

proposals.title: Computational Dissociation of Reward and Non-Reward Prediction Error Signals in Medial vs. Lateral Orbitofrontal Cortex in Major Depressive Disorder
proposals.motivation: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) is characterized by profound anhedonia and distorted reward processing, yet the precise neural computations underlying these deficits remain unclear. Pre-2026 work by Rolls and others has established the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) as critical for representing reward value and outcome expectancies, with a functional gradient from medial (reward) to lateral (non-reward/punishment) regions. Computational psychiatry offers a framework to formally test how these value-coding mechanisms break down in MDD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals with MDD will exhibit a specific computational imbalance, characterized by blunted reward prediction error (RPE) signals in medial OFC and exaggerated non-reward prediction error (NRPE) signals in lateral OFC, relative to healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will combine fMRI with a probabilistic reinforcement learning task designed to orthogonally manipulate reward and non-reward outcomes. Using a Rescorla-Wagner model and a modified Q-learning algorithm that separately tracks expected values for reward and non-reward, we will generate trial-by-trial estimates of RPE and NRPE. These parametric regressors will be used in a general linear model to identify their unique neural correlates in medial and lateral OFC subdivisions, comparing unmedicated MDD patients (n=30) to matched healthy controls (n=30).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a formal computational account of value coding abnormalities in the OFC in MDD, moving beyond descriptive neuroimaging to a mechanistic model. It will directly test Rolls' theoretical framework on OFC functional specialization in a clinical population, potentially identifying novel, circuit-specific biomarkers for anhedonia.
proposals.novelty: While RL models have been applied to MDD, few studies have explicitly modeled non-reward prediction errors or tested for their dissociation within OFC subregions as predicted by pre-2026 primate neurophysiology and human neuroimaging work led by Rolls.

proposals.title: Network Control Theory of Orbitofrontal Cortex Subregions: Modelling State Transitions from Depressive to Healthy Brain Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Major depressive disorder (MDD) is linked to dysfunctional reward processing and altered functional connectivity, particularly involving the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC), a key region for reward valuation and emotion. Pre-2026, network control theory (NCT) has emerged as a powerful framework for modelling how brain regions drive state transitions, but it has not been systematically applied to OFC subregions (e.g., medial vs. lateral) to understand their differential roles in shifting brain-wide dynamics from a depressive to a healthy state. This limits the theoretical basis for targeted neuromodulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific OFC subregions, due to their distinct anatomical connectivity profiles, will exhibit differential controllability properties, with the medial OFC acting as a key 'driver' for transitioning the brain from a depressive network state (characterized by hypoactivity in reward circuits) to a healthy one.
proposals.method: We will construct a structural connectome from the Human Connectome Project (pre-2026) and assign regional dynamics based on a reduced Wong-Wang model. We will define a 'depressive' state as reduced activity in ventral striatum and anterior cingulate cortex, and a 'healthy' state as their normalized activity. Using NCT, we will compute average and modal controllability for each OFC subregion. We will then simulate optimal stimulation trajectories from the depressive to the healthy state using minimum energy control, identifying which OFC subregions require minimal energy input to drive this transition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based account of how OFC subcircuits could differentially control whole-brain state transitions relevant to MDD. It will generate testable predictions for targeted brain stimulation (e.g., TMS, DBS) by identifying optimal control nodes. The project bridges Rolls's theories on reward systems and scene representation with modern computational network neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: While NCT has been applied to macro-scale brain networks, its application to model the specific control roles of OFC subregions in driving transitions between clinically defined brain states is novel. It uniquely integrates Rolls's detailed neurobiological understanding of OFC function with a cutting-edge control-theoretic framework.

proposals.title: A Computational Model of the Continuous Cortico-Hippocampal Loop in the Gradual Consolidation of Episodic and Scene Memory
proposals.motivation: The standard systems consolidation model posits a one-time transfer of memory from hippocampus to neocortex. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests a continuous, reciprocal dialogue between these regions, which is not well understood. This project addresses a critical gap by modeling how this persistent loop, rather than a single transfer, supports the gradual transformation and stabilization of long-term memories, particularly for complex scenes and events central to Rolls's work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous, reciprocal neocortex-hippocampus-neocortex loop implements a form of interleaved replay and synaptic renormalization, enabling episodic and scene memories to be gradually consolidated into neocortical semantic and spatial frameworks without catastrophic interference.
proposals.method: We will develop a neurobiologically constrained computational model. The architecture will feature a continuous attractor network for the neocortical representation (e.g., of scenes or objects) and a pattern separation/completion network for the hippocampus. The core algorithm will implement offline, interleaved replay between these modules, governed by a hippocampal indexing mechanism. Consolidation will be driven by a synaptic trace model (e.g., cascaded weight changes) in the neocortex, with the hippocampus providing consistent retrieval cues. The model will be tested on tasks of sequential scene learning and memory recall over simulated time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable computational framework for continuous systems consolidation, linking Rolls's theories on hippocampal spatial view cells and neocortical scene representation to memory dynamics. It will generate specific predictions about replay timing and neocortical representational change that can be tested in future neurophysiological experiments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling consolidation as a perpetual, interactive loop rather than a sequential handoff, and in integrating mechanisms for preserving episodic detail while building neocortical schemas, a key challenge not resolved by standard complementary learning systems (CLS) models.

proposals.title: A Multi-Area Cortical Model of Scene Representation Integrating Ventral, Dorsal, and Medial Prefrontal Streams for Reward-Guided Navigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 computational models of scene representation, including Rolls's work on hippocampal spatial view cells, often focus on isolated 'What' (ventral stream) and 'Where' (dorsal stream) pathways. However, real-world behavior, such as reward-guided navigation in complex scenes, requires the dynamic integration of these streams with memory and motivational systems. A comprehensive, multi-area cortical model is needed to understand how these distributed systems interact to produce coherent perception and action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical, attractor network model incorporating interacting ventral (IT), dorsal (PPC), medial prefrontal (vmPFC/OFC), and hippocampal (CA1/subiculum) regions will generate more robust and behaviorally relevant scene representations than models of isolated pathways, by simulating the convergence of object identity, spatial location, and reward value.
proposals.method: We will construct a large-scale, neuroanatomically constrained model using integrate-and-fire attractor networks, as previously developed by Rolls for memory systems. Specific modules will simulate: 1) Ventral stream (V1-V2-V4-IT) for object identity, 2) Dorsal stream (V1-MT-PPC) for spatial view and motion, 3) Hippocampal formation for allocentric scene context, and 4) Orbitofrontal/ventromedial prefrontal cortex for reward value. Connectivity will be based on pre-2026 tract-tracing data. Learning will be implemented via spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) and reward-modulated Hebbian rules to train associations between streams.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated computational model of how multiple neocortical regions and the hippocampus interact to form scene representations that guide goal-directed behavior. It will generate testable predictions for neuroimaging and electrophysiology regarding the flow of information between object, spatial, and value circuits during navigation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While previous models have simulated individual pathways, the novelty lies in the explicit, large-scale integration of the ventral, dorsal, and reward valuation streams within a single, biologically plausible framework, directly extending Rolls's theory of scene representation from the hippocampus to the full cortical hierarchy.

proposals.title: A Minimal Hippocampal-Cortical Circuit for the Episodic Recall of Spatial Scenes from Viewpoint Cues
proposals.motivation: The hippocampus is critical for episodic memory, but its complex, multi-regional architecture makes it difficult to isolate the core circuit for a specific function. Pre-2026 work by Rolls and others has established that spatial view cells in the primate hippocampus provide a neural substrate for scene representation and memory. However, the minimal subcircuit within the hippocampal formation and its cortical inputs sufficient to achieve cue-triggered recall of a full scene representation remains undefined, hindering mechanistic understanding and computational modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core function of recalling a coherent spatial scene from a partial visual viewpoint cue can be achieved by a minimal circuit centered on the reciprocal connectivity between the entorhinal cortex (providing perceptual input) and hippocampal CA1 (integrating and recalling spatial representations), bypassing the traditional trisynaptic loop.
proposals.method: We will construct a biologically constrained computational model using integrate-and-fire neurons with synaptic adaptation (STDP). The architecture will feature a simplified ventral visual stream (V4, PPA) projecting to lateral entorhinal cortex, and a parietal stream projecting to medial entorhinal cortex. These converge on a reduced hippocampal network focusing on the direct perforant path to CA1 and the reciprocal CA1-to-entorhinal feedback. We will train the model using backpropagation-through-time and contrast its scene recall performance and neural dynamics (simulating spatial view cell emergence) against a full trisynaptic circuit model and lesion studies in non-human primates.
proposals.contribution: This work will propose a parsimonious, testable circuit model for a core hippocampal memory function, directly linking Rolls's theory of scene representation to a minimal implementable architecture. It will generate specific predictions for future electrophysiological and optogenetic experiments by identifying which connections are essential for recall. Furthermore, it will provide a foundational model for understanding how disruptions in this minimal circuit could underlie memory deficits linked to trauma or disease.
proposals.novelty: While previous models incorporate the full hippocampal trisynaptic loop, this proposal uniquely seeks to *downscale* to a minimal sufficient circuit for a defined cognitive function (scene recall), challenging the necessity of canonical pathways like the dentate gyrus/CA3 for this specific operation. It integrates Rolls's spatial view cell framework with a reductionist circuit analysis not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Inferring Hippocampal Retrieval Cues from Neocortical Recall Patterns: A Computational Model of Inverse Episodic Memory
proposals.motivation: The hippocampal-neocortical dialogue is central to memory recall, where a sparse hippocampal cue triggers pattern completion in the neocortex. However, the inverse problem—determining what hippocampal cue could have elicited a given recalled neocortical state—remains unaddressed. Solving this is crucial for understanding memory distortions, false recalls, and the computational principles of bidirectional hippocampal-neocortical communication, as outlined in Rolls's theories of scene representation and episodic memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a trained inverse mapping, from a high-dimensional neocortical representation (e.g., of a scene or object) back to a sparse, structured hippocampal spatial view cell pattern, can be learned and will reveal the minimal, cue-like features sufficient to trigger a specific recall.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep autoencoder architecture where the encoder (simulating forward recall) maps a sparse hippocampal spatial view cell vector (based on Rolls's model) through a multi-layer perceptron to a detailed neocortical visual/scene representation. The critical inverse model will be a separate, trained decoder network that takes the neocortical pattern as input and outputs a predicted hippocampal cue. We will train and test the model using synthetic datasets of paired hippocampal (sparse, allocentric) and neocortical (dense, featural) patterns, employing gradient descent and regularization to enforce sparsity in the predicted hippocampal output.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework for the inverse problem in hippocampal-neocortical recall, formally linking recalled content to potential retrieval cues. It will offer a novel tool for theorizing about memory errors and the stability of recall, directly extending Rolls's work on hippocampal spatial view cells and cortical memory systems.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of recall are established, the explicit inversion of this process to infer a plausible hippocampal cue from cortical activity is a novel and non-obvious direction, moving beyond description to a predictive, generative theory of memory cueing.

proposals.title: A Common Hippocampal Code for Spatial, Reward, and Semantic Associations: Testing the Unity of Declarative Memory
proposals.motivation: The dominant view in cognitive neuroscience posits functionally independent memory systems, with the hippocampus specialized for spatial and episodic memory, separate from reward valuation and semantic processing. However, Rolls' work on spatial view cells and reward-related hippocampal activity suggests a more integrative role. This project challenges the pure dissociation model by testing if the hippocampus implements a common associative algorithm across memory domains, a critical question for understanding its fundamental computational principle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the human hippocampus employs a shared computational process—specifically, continuous attractor dynamics for pattern completion and separation—to form associative representations for spatial scenes, reward outcomes, and verbal-semantic relationships, indicating a core declarative memory mechanism.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution 7T fMRI in healthy participants performing three tasks within-scanner: a virtual navigation task (spatial), a probabilistic reversal learning task (reward), and a paired-associate word learning task (verbal). We will apply Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) to test for common representational geometries across task domains within hippocampal subfields (CA1, CA3, DG). We will then train a computational model, a continuous attractor network with Hebbian plasticity, on the multi-modal task data to simulate hippocampal activity and test if a single network can reproduce the observed fMRI patterns across all three memory types.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct empirical test of the unity versus modularity of hippocampal memory functions. It will integrate Rolls' theories on scene representation and reward into a broader model of declarative memory. The findings could shift theoretical frameworks from multiple independent systems to a core associative system with domain-general inputs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly comparing spatial, reward, and verbal memory representations within the same individuals using RSA and modeling them with a single attractor network architecture, moving beyond domain-specific investigations to test a unifying hippocampal computation.

proposals.title: Effective Connectivity of Medial Temporal and Prefrontal Cortical Networks Underlying Word-Pair Episodic Memory
proposals.motivation: Professor Rolls's prior work has delineated effective connectivity networks for object-location and reward-location memory, key components of episodic memory. However, a parallel, detailed analysis for verbal associative memory (e.g., word-pair learning) is lacking. Completing this analysis is crucial for a comprehensive, systems-level understanding of episodic memory, as it will reveal whether core medial temporal lobe circuits are task-invariant or if semantic/linguistic processing recruits distinct prefrontal and temporal cortical pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful encoding and retrieval of word-pairs will engage a core effective connectivity network involving the hippocampus, perirhinal cortex, and medial prefrontal cortex, similar to visuospatial tasks, but with significantly strengthened influences from language-related regions (e.g., left inferior frontal gyrus and lateral temporal cortex) compared to object-location memory networks.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing fMRI data from a word-pair episodic memory task using Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI, a pre-2026 standard for effective connectivity. A biologically plausible model space will be constructed, centered on the hippocampus, perirhinal cortex, parahippocampal cortex, medial prefrontal cortex, and left-hemisphere language regions (inferior frontal gyrus, middle temporal gyrus). Bayesian Model Selection and Averaging will be used to identify the most likely network architecture and compare the strength and direction of connections (i.e., effective connectivity) directly with previously published DCM results from object-location and reward-location tasks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct comparison of effective connectivity architectures across key episodic memory domains within the same theoretical and methodological framework. It will specify how language systems integrate with core hippocampal-prefrontal memory circuits. The findings will refine computational models of episodic memory by constraining how different information types (verbal vs. visuospatial) flow through the medial temporal lobe system.
proposals.novelty: While DCM has been applied to memory, a direct comparative connectivity analysis between verbal associative memory and the well-characterized visuospatial/reward networks studied by Rolls's group is novel. It tests the generality of previously identified circuits and explicitly models the integration of semantic/linguistic processing into the hippocampal memory network.

proposals.title: Neural Systems for Episodic Memory Diversity: An fMRI Investigation of Temporal Order and Social Event Representations in the Human Hippocampus and Cortex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Rolls and others has established the role of the hippocampus and connected cortical regions in specific episodic memory types, such as spatial scene and object-in-place memory. However, a comprehensive, systems-level understanding of how the brain encodes other fundamental dimensions of episodic experience—specifically temporal sequence and social context—is lacking. Testing these memory types is crucial for developing a complete neural theory of episodic memory that aligns with its multifaceted nature in human cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct but overlapping neural systems within the medial temporal lobe and associated cortical regions (e.g., prefrontal cortex, posterior cingulate) will be differentially engaged during the retrieval of temporal order memories versus social event memories, extending beyond the networks identified for spatial and object-based episodic memory.
proposals.method: We will design a novel, ecologically valid fMRI paradigm where participants encode and retrieve complex video vignettes. The paradigm will include conditions for: 1) Temporal Order Memory (judging the sequence of events), and 2) Social Event Memory (recalling who said/did what to whom). Using a 3T fMRI scanner, we will employ a block and event-related design. Data analysis will involve multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) to decode memory type and identify functional connectivity between the hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and temporoparietal junction, regions implicated in social cognition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct fMRI comparison of the neural substrates for temporal and social episodic memory within a single paradigm, mapping them onto Rolls's theoretical framework of hippocampal-cortical interactions. It will generate a more nuanced model of how different episodic memory features are integrated, informing theories of memory organization and disorders like Alzheimer's disease where social and temporal memory are impaired.
proposals.novelty: While temporal and social memory have been studied separately, systematically contrasting them using a unified fMRI paradigm grounded in Rolls's scene representation theory is novel. The approach directly tests the generality of hippocampal spatial view cell models to non-spatial, yet fundamental, dimensions of episodic experience.

proposals.title: Longitudinal fMRI and Computational Modeling of Cortical-Hippocampal Network Dynamics for Episodic and Spatial Scene Memory in Healthy Aging
proposals.motivation: Aging is associated with a decline in episodic and spatial memory, functions critically dependent on the hippocampus and its cortical interactions, particularly with the parietal and medial prefrontal cortex. However, pre-2026 longitudinal studies tracking how these specific functional networks evolve over time are scarce. Understanding the differential vulnerability or plasticity of networks subserving distinct memory types (e.g., reward-associated episodic vs. pure spatial scene memory) is crucial for developing targeted cognitive interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that with healthy aging, the functional connectivity supporting reward-modulated episodic memory will show earlier and more pronounced decline compared to the network for allocentric spatial scene representation, and that targeted cognitive training will induce selective reconfiguration and strengthening of these dissociable cortical-hippocampal circuits.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 3-year longitudinal study with 120 participants (aged 50-70). Using task-based and resting-state fMRI, we will employ a paradigm differentiating reward-related episodic memory (engaging orbitofrontal-hippocampal circuits) and spatial scene memory (engaging parahippocampal-parietal-hippocampal circuits). We will use dynamic causal modeling (DCM) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to quantify effective connectivity changes. Participants will be randomized into active cognitive training (focusing on either memory type) or control groups, with scans at baseline, 18 months, and 36 months.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a longitudinal map of how distinct cortical-hippocampal memory networks change with age, directly testing Rolls' theories on separable systems for scene and reward-influenced memory. It will identify which networks are most plastic in response to targeted training, offering a mechanistic basis for personalized cognitive maintenance strategies and biomarkers for resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the longitudinal, within-subject design that directly compares two theoretically dissociable memory networks defined by Rolls' pre-2026 work, combined with an interventional component to test network-specific plasticity, moving beyond cross-sectional correlation to causal understanding of network trajectory.

proposals.title: Neural Resource Competition During Encoding: An fMRI Study of Interference Between Hippocampal Spatial, Reward, and Verbal Memory Systems
proposals.motivation: Edmund Rolls's work has established distinct brain systems for spatial view (hippocampus), reward (orbitofrontal cortex), and semantic processing (lateral temporal cortex). A key unresolved question is how these systems interact and compete for limited cognitive resources during real-time encoding, which is central to understanding memory integration and failures in conditions like PTSD or addiction. Pre-2026 dual-task literature suggests interference, but the neural substrates of this competition remain poorly mapped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that concurrent spatial, reward, and verbal encoding tasks will produce behavioral interference and mutually suppressive fMRI BOLD signals in their respective core neural systems (hippocampus, orbitofrontal cortex, and lateral temporal cortex), demonstrating direct neural resource competition.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subjects fMRI design with carefully matched dual-task paradigms. Participants will encode spatial scenes, reward-associated stimuli, and verbal items in single and concurrent pairings. Behavioral performance (accuracy, RT) will be measured. fMRI data will be analyzed using SPM12, with a focus on psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to test for task-dependent functional connectivity changes between regions of interest (hippocampus, OFC, inferior temporal gyrus). Multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) will assess representational fidelity under interference.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct neural signature of resource competition between major memory systems, moving beyond behavioral correlations. It will test and extend Rolls's theoretical framework on separable memory systems by quantifying their interactive dynamics. The findings could inform models of cognitive load and resilience in trauma, where such systems may be imbalanced.
proposals.novelty: While interference is well-studied behaviorally, simultaneously probing competition between these three specific, well-defined neural systems (spatial, reward, verbal) with fMRI and connectivity analyses is novel. It directly tests a systems-level prediction from Rolls's scene and reward models that has not been empirically combined in this way.

proposals.title: Graph-Theoretic Characterization of Episodic Memory Networks: A Connectomic Analysis of Hippocampal-Cortical Interactions for Scene and Reward Processing
proposals.motivation: Edmund Rolls's pre-2026 work has established the hippocampus's role in spatial scene memory via view cells and its interaction with reward systems. However, the precise network architecture supporting distinct episodic memory components—spatial, emotional, and reward-related—remains poorly characterized at a whole-brain level. Applying modern network neuroscience tools can bridge the gap between localized neural mechanisms and large-scale system dynamics, a key challenge in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that episodic memory for scenes with emotional or reward content is supported by a distinct, reconfigurable brain graph, identifiable by specific graph theory metrics (e.g., modularity, hub strength) that differentiate it from networks for neutral scene processing or general reward evaluation.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution resting-state and task-based fMRI data from the Human Connectome Project and related pre-2026 datasets. Using established pipelines (e.g., FSL, CONN toolbox), we will construct whole-brain structural and functional connectivity matrices. Graph theory metrics (e.g., betweenness centrality, participation coefficient, small-worldness) will be computed for networks defined by key regions from Rolls's models: hippocampus, parahippocampal gyrus, orbitofrontal cortex, and amygdala. Statistical comparisons will be made between memory conditions using network-based statistics (NBS).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, network-level model of how hippocampal spatial representations integrate with cortical reward and emotional systems to form episodic memories. It will directly test and extend Rolls's theoretical framework by identifying the specific connectivity patterns that confer resilience or vulnerability in memory networks, particularly in relation to individual differences in reward sensitivity or trauma history.
proposals.novelty: While connectomics has been applied to memory, this proposal uniquely integrates Rolls's specific neurobiological models of scene and reward processing with whole-brain graph theory, moving beyond correlational maps to a formal characterization of the episodic memory system as a distinct, reconfigurable brain graph.

proposals.title: Primacy of Affective and Somatic Associations in Scene Processing: A Test of Non-Visual Primacy Using Intracranial Recordings
proposals.motivation: The dominant model of visual processing, including Rolls's own work on scene representation via hippocampal spatial view cells, posits a hierarchy where complex visual categorization precedes associative activation in other modalities. This assumption may be incomplete for understanding rapid, real-world perception where affective and somatic associations are critical for survival and decision-making. Testing this could redefine models of how the brain integrates sensory and reward information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for ecologically salient stimuli (e.g., threatening scenes, rewarding objects), associative activations in brain regions linked to affect (amygdala, orbitofrontal cortex) and somatic representation (somatosensory cortex) can precede or occur independently of explicit visual category recognition in the ventral visual stream.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-temporal-resolution intracranial EEG (iEEG) and single-neuron recordings from pre-surgical epilepsy patients, focusing on regions of interest: ventral visual stream (e.g., inferior temporal cortex), somatosensory cortex, amygdala, and orbitofrontal cortex. Participants will view rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) of images varying in emotional valence and category. We will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and Granger causality on the neural time series to determine the latency and causal direction of category-specific visual decoding versus affective/somatic associative decoding.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct empirical evidence for or against the primacy of visual processing in associative brain networks. It will extend Rolls's theory of scene representation by specifying conditions under which non-visual information drives or precedes visual categorization, offering a more integrated model of perception, emotion, and motivation.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly inverts a core assumption in visual neuroscience by seeking evidence that associative, non-visual signals can lead visual categorization, a possibility largely overlooked in pre-2026 hierarchical models. It uniquely combines iEEG temporal resolution with Rolls's theoretical framework on scene and reward processing.

proposals.title: Individual Differences in Category-Selective Cortical Networks: Linking Functional Connectivity to Reward History and Scene Representation
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging research has largely assumed a universal, canonical organization of category-selective regions (e.g., FFA, PPA). However, pre-2026 work by Rolls and others highlights the role of reward learning in shaping visual representations and the individual-specific nature of hippocampal spatial view cell responses. This suggests that category networks may be sculpted by personal experience, creating meaningful individual differences that are currently overlooked.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength and anatomical location of functional connectivity between category-selective visual regions (e.g., PPA for scenes) and reward-related regions (e.g., orbitofrontal cortex, ventral striatum) will vary systematically across individuals, and that this variability will be predicted by individual differences in scene-related reward history and navigational experience.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution 7T fMRI data from the Human Connectome Project and in-house datasets. Participants will undergo resting-state and task-based scans (scene viewing, reward tasks). Using FSL and CONN toolbox, we will first identify individual-level category peaks for scenes and faces. We will then compute seed-based functional connectivity from these individual peaks. Individual differences in connectivity strength will be modeled using generalized psychophysiological interaction (gPPI) analyses and correlated with behavioral measures of scene preference, navigation ability, and reward sensitivity derived from pre-scan questionnaires and task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, experience-driven framework for understanding the functional architecture of the visual cortex, moving beyond group-averaged maps. It will directly link Rolls's theories of reward-guided vision and hippocampal scene representation to observable variability in large-scale cortical networks, offering a mechanistic bridge between systems neuroscience and individual cognitive traits.
proposals.novelty: While individual localization is known, systematically linking the *functional connectivity* of individually-defined category regions to reward and experiential variables is novel. It integrates Rolls's work on reward, memory, and scene representation into a unified model of individual brain organization.

proposals.title: Dynamic Reconfiguration of Functional Connectivity for Scene and Face Processing During a Continuous Viewing Task
proposals.motivation: Functional connectivity (FC) is typically computed over entire task blocks, assuming stationarity. However, Rolls's work on hippocampal spatial view cells and cortical scene representations suggests that neural representations are dynamic and task-dependent. Testing the stationarity assumption is crucial for understanding how brain regions like the hippocampus, parahippocampal cortex, and reward-related orbitofrontal cortex interact in real-time to support perception and memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that FC patterns between core visual, scene-processing (e.g., parahippocampal place area), and reward-related (e.g., orbitofrontal cortex) regions will show significant, systematic non-stationarity within a single session, specifically modulated by the category (scenes vs. human faces) and the evolving motivational state of the participant.
proposals.method: We will collect high-temporal-resolution fMRI data while participants perform a prolonged, motivationally engaging viewing task with alternating blocks of complex scenes and human faces. Using pre-2026 dynamic connectivity methods (e.g., sliding-window correlation, time-frequency coherence analysis), we will quantify moment-to-moment FC fluctuations. We will employ Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) to identify discrete, recurring FC states and relate state transitions to stimulus category, time-on-task, and behavioral measures of reward (e.g., subjective pleasantness ratings).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly challenge the standard stationary FC model, providing a novel, dynamic account of how brain systems for perception, memory, and reward interact in real-time. It will bridge Rolls's theories on scene representation and emotion/motivation by showing how their underlying neural circuits are temporally coordinated, offering a more accurate model for understanding resilience and psychopathology linked to trauma.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic FC is studied in resting-state, applying it to a controlled, category-specific perceptual task within Rolls's theoretical framework of scene/reward processing is novel. It moves beyond describing *what* regions are connected to model *how* and *when* these connections reconfigure during motivated behavior.

proposals.title: Dynamic Visual Scenes and Reward: A Computational Neuroscience Investigation of Motion-Enhanced Cortical and Hippocampal Representations
proposals.motivation: Edmund Rolls's pre-2026 work establishes that the hippocampus encodes scenes via spatial view cells and that reward value is linked to visual representations in the orbitofrontal cortex. However, most foundational studies use static images, which poorly engage motion-processing regions like MT/V5 and associated parietal cortices. Understanding how dynamic, naturalistic stimuli modulate scene representation and its coupling with reward systems is critical for a complete model of real-world perception and memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dynamic visual stimuli, compared to matched static frames, will elicit stronger and more distributed neural representations in motion-sensitive and association cortices, leading to enhanced functional connectivity between these regions, the hippocampus (for scene memory), and the orbitofrontal cortex (for reward valuation).
proposals.method: We will employ a multimodal approach combining human fMRI and computational modeling. Participants will view matched dynamic (short videos) and static (key frames) scenes of natural environments and human social interactions during fMRI. We will use multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode scene identity and representational similarity analysis (RSA) to compare neural representations to a deep neural network model (e.g., a 3D CNN like I3D or SlowFast) trained on action recognition. Dynamic causal modeling (DCM) will be used to assess effective connectivity between regions of interest including V1, MT/V5, parahippocampal cortex, hippocampus, and orbitofrontal cortex.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic account of how motion information enriches scene representations beyond static features. It will directly extend Rolls's theories of scene processing and reward by identifying the cortical pathways that integrate dynamic visual input with spatial memory and affective value. The findings will bridge gaps between the study of static object/scene perception and the dynamic, real-world contexts in which the brain naturally operates.
proposals.novelty: While the roles of static scene-selective regions are well-studied, systematically comparing dynamic vs. static inputs within Rolls's established theoretical framework of hippocampal spatial view cells and reward systems is novel. The integration of action-recognition deep learning models with neural data to explain dynamic scene coding represents a significant methodological advance for cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: The Cortical Architecture of Reward and Scene Processing: Integration of Task-Evoked Category Maps with Intrinsic Resting-State Networks
proposals.motivation: The human brain's functional architecture is characterized by both stable intrinsic connectivity networks (ICNs) and specialized, task-evoked systems for processing categories like scenes, faces, and reward. Pre-2026 work, including the Human Connectome Project (HCP) atlas, has mapped these systems largely in isolation. Understanding how the dynamic, category-specific activations studied by Rolls and others (e.g., for scenes and reward) are constrained by and emerge from the brain's intrinsic, stable connectome is a fundamental problem in systems neuroscience, bridging perception, memory, and motivation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the spatial topography and functional connectivity of task-defined category-selective networks (for scenes, humans/faces, and reward) will show a systematic and non-random alignment with specific canonical ICNs (e.g., the dorsal attention, default mode, and salience/ventral attention networks), reflecting a principled embedding of specialized processing within the brain's intrinsic architecture.
proposals.method: We will utilize the pre-processed HCP 1200-subject dataset. Task-evoked functional networks for visual categories (scenes, faces) and reward-related processing will be defined using GLM-based contrasts from the HCP task-fMRI data. Intrinsic connectivity networks will be derived from resting-state data using group-ICA as implemented in FSL. Spatial correspondence will be quantified using voxel-wise spatial correlation and Dice overlap metrics. Functional integration will be assessed by comparing task-evoked functional connectivity patterns with resting-state connectivity profiles of the aligned ICNs, using seed-based correlation and multivariate pattern analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified model of how evolutionarily and developmentally shaped intrinsic connectivity scaffolds the brain's capacity for specialized, goal-directed computations. It will directly extend Rolls's theoretical frameworks on scene representation and reward by grounding them in the brain's intrinsic network organization, offering a more complete systems-level explanation.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared task activation to resting networks, a focused, quantitative integration of the specific category and reward systems central to Rolls's research with the full HCP ICN atlas is novel. This moves beyond mere localization to explain the network origins of these specialized functions.

proposals.title: Deep Neural Network Models of Vision Predict Scene and Human-Representation Responses in the Primate Ventral Visual Stream and Associated Cortical Regions
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the brain transforms visual input into rich, behaviorally relevant representations of scenes and humans is a central challenge in systems neuroscience. Pre-2026, deep neural networks (DNNs) trained on object recognition have emerged as powerful models of the ventral visual stream, but their ability to predict neural activity in associative cortical regions involved in scene understanding, social perception, and reward-related processing remains less explored. This gap limits our computational understanding of how high-level visual and semantic features are constructed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that features extracted from deep layers of biologically inspired DNNs (e.g., convolutional neural networks and scene-specific architectures) will significantly predict neural responses in the macaque inferior temporal visual cortex (IT), parahippocampal gyrus, and reward-related orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) to complex visual stimuli, particularly scenes containing humans.
proposals.method: We will record single-neuron and local field potential data from macaque IT, parahippocampal cortex, and OFC during presentation of complex naturalistic images and videos featuring scenes and humans. We will extract feature representations from multiple layers of pre-trained DNNs, including VGG-19, ResNet-50, and a scene classification network (Place-CNN). Using linear and kernelized ridge regression, we will build mapping models from DNN features to neural activity. We will specifically test whether scene- and human-selective DNN units best predict activity in parahippocampal and social perception regions, respectively, and whether OFC responses are predicted by features linked to reward valence in the stimuli.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, model-based link between artificial vision systems and high-level associative cortex, extending the success of DNN models beyond core object recognition. It will test the theory that scene representations are built from hierarchical visual features and reveal how visual information is transformed into representations with motivational and social significance, bridging Rolls's work on vision, scene processing, and reward.
proposals.novelty: While DNNs have been used to model IT, applying them to simultaneously predict activity in the visual, scene-related (parahippocampal), and reward-related (OFC) cortex within a unified framework is novel. It directly integrates Rolls's theories on spatial view cells and reward with state-of-the-art (pre-2026) computational vision models.

proposals.title: Decoding Visual Category from Functional Connectivity Patterns in Ventral Visual and Reward-Related Cortical Networks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Rolls and others has established that specific brain regions, such as the ventral visual stream and orbitofrontal cortex, are critical for representing object categories and their reward value. However, most decoding studies focus on activation patterns within regions. The 'inverse problem' of predicting stimulus category from the dynamic functional connectivity *between* these regions remains largely unexplored, yet is crucial for understanding how distributed networks integrate visual and affective information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pattern of task-evoked functional connectivity between the ventral visual stream, parahippocampal scene area, hippocampus, and orbitofrontal cortex contains sufficient information to decode the category (e.g., faces, scenes, rewarding objects) of a viewed stimulus, and that this connectivity-based code is distinct from and complementary to activation-based codes.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new human fMRI data where participants view images from distinct categories (faces, scenes, objects). Using pre-2026 methods, we will compute trial-wise functional connectivity matrices (e.g., using psychophysiological interaction, PPI, or beta-series correlation) between predefined regions of interest. These connectivity patterns will serve as features for classification using support vector machines (SVMs) and regularized linear models. We will compare decoding accuracy from connectivity features to that from traditional multi-voxel activation patterns and use model interpretation techniques (e.g., weight mapping) to identify the most informative connections for each category.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel methodological framework for decoding cognitive states from network interactions, directly extending Rolls's theories on scene representation and reward processing. It will provide empirical evidence for how category-specific information is embedded in inter-regional communication, offering a systems-level complement to single-region representational models.
proposals.novelty: While decoding from activation is standard, predicting stimulus category purely from the *functional connectivity pattern* evoked by a single stimulus trial is a significant and non-obvious inversion of the typical analytical approach, moving the unit of analysis from regional activity to the network configuration.

proposals.title: Laminar-Specific fMRI of Scene and Human Category Processing in the Human Medial Temporal Lobe and Reward-Related Cortex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Rolls and others has identified distinct neural systems for processing visual scenes (involving parahippocampal cortex and hippocampal spatial view cells) and human faces/bodies (involving superior temporal sulcus), and how these representations interface with reward systems. However, the laminar organization of these category-specific computations in humans remains unknown, limiting our understanding of cortical microcircuitry for perception, memory, and motivation. Ultra-high field (7T) fMRI now enables layer-specific functional imaging to address this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that category-specific processing for scenes and humans engages distinct feedforward and feedback information flow patterns across cortical layers in key regions, and that reward value modulates these patterns specifically in deep layers of orbitofrontal cortex.
proposals.method: We will use 7T fMRI with sub-millimeter resolution (0.8 mm isotropic) to acquire data from regions of interest (hippocampus, parahippocampal cortex, fusiform face area, superior temporal sulcus, orbitofrontal cortex). Participants will view images of scenes, human faces, and objects, some paired with monetary reward. We will employ a multi-echo EPI sequence for improved signal and layer-specific analysis using the layer-fMRI toolbox (LayNii). Statistical analysis will use general linear models (GLM) within each cortical layer, and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) will be applied to infer laminar-specific effective connectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence in humans for how cortical layers implement category-specific visual and reward processing, bridging Rolls's theoretical models of scene representation and emotion with cortical microcircuitry. It will establish a laminar-functional map for key nodes in the visual and reward hierarchies, informing models of hierarchical predictive coding and memory formation.
proposals.novelty: While layer-fMRI is emerging, its application to test specific hypotheses about category processing and reward modulation in the human medial temporal lobe and orbitofrontal cortex, based on detailed neurophysiological models from non-human primates, is novel and non-obvious.

proposals.title: Deactivation and Network Reconfiguration During Visual Categorization: A Cortical-Hippocampal Perspective
proposals.motivation: Neuroimaging research has predominantly focused on task-induced activations, while systematic deactivations and reduced functional connectivity are less understood. In the context of visual and scene processing, understanding these 'negative' signals is crucial, as they may reflect active suppression of default-mode or competing networks to optimize resource allocation for goal-directed behavior, a core principle in Rolls's theories of cortical function and reward.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during demanding visual categorization tasks, specific cortical regions (e.g., default mode network nodes) will show consistent deactivation, and their connectivity with task-positive regions (e.g., ventral visual stream, prefrontal cortex) will decrease, and that the magnitude of this network reconfiguration will correlate with behavioral performance and be modulated by reward contingency.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly acquired fMRI data from participants performing visual categorization tasks (faces, scenes, objects) versus baseline. Using SPM12 or FSL for general linear modeling, we will identify regions showing significant deactivation. We will then perform psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses and graph-theoretical measures on time-series data to quantify task-induced decreases in functional connectivity. The role of the hippocampus and related cortical regions in scene-specific deactivation will be a focus, based on Rolls's spatial view cell model.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a systematic map of deactivation and connectivity reduction during categorization, linking it to established cortical hierarchies and hippocampal function. It will offer a more complete neural model of cognitive processing by integrating 'negative' signals with activations, directly testing predictions from Rolls's frameworks on cortical computation, memory systems, and motivated behavior.
proposals.novelty: While deactivations are noted, this proposal uniquely frames them as an active component of network reconfiguration for efficient processing, explicitly tying them to Rolls's integrative theories of vision, memory, and reward, and using connectivity decreases as a key mechanistic metric beyond simple BOLD signal change.

proposals.title: The Phenotypic Manifold of Cortical Noise: Linking High-Dimensional Organismal Traits to the Covariance Structure of Multi-Region Neural Variability
proposals.motivation: Current models of large-scale cortical network dynamics often treat trial-to-trial neural variability as intrinsic 'noise', typically modeled as independent across regions or as low-dimensional shared fluctuations. This fails to account for the organism's holistic state. Pre-2026 work on resilience to trauma and scene representation highlights how brain systems integrate multi-modal information, suggesting that the dominant sources of variability may be externally structured by the organism's complete phenotypic profile, not by independent neural processes.
proposals.hypothesis: The covariance structure of spontaneous and task-evoked neural variability across multiple cortical and hippocampal regions is not random but is systematically predicted by the high-dimensional correlations within an organism's phenome, with collinear phenotypic traits generating a low-dimensional manifold of shared network constraints.
proposals.method: We will integrate large-scale neural recordings (e.g., Neuropixels) from macaque cortical and hippocampal regions involved in scene processing and reward (e.g., PFC, IT, hippocampus) with a comprehensive phenomic database (~2,291 factors including metabolic, anatomical, behavioral, and genetic markers). Using canonical correlation analysis (CCA) and partial least squares regression (PLSR), we will model the mapping from phenotypic covariance matrices to neural activity covariance matrices. We will then employ manifold learning techniques (e.g., diffusion maps) to identify the low-dimensional phenotypic structure that best predicts the dominant modes of shared neural variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, integrative framework for understanding neural variability, moving beyond the graded vs. spiking noise dichotomy. It will directly test whether the statistical architecture of the phenome is a primary determinant of brain-wide activity patterns, offering a new lens through which to interpret individual differences in cognition, resilience, and brain disorders.
proposals.novelty: This approach is non-obvious because it inverts the standard model, proposing that the phenome structures neural noise, rather than treating noise as an intrinsic property of neural circuits. It fuses Rolls's work on scene representation and resilience with high-dimensional phenotyping and covariance modeling in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Computational Network Psychiatry: A Noisy Attractor Network Model of Suicide Risk Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Suicide is a leading cause of death, yet its prediction remains poor due to complex, dynamic interactions between psychological and neurobiological risk factors. Pre-2026 network medicine conceptualizes psychiatric disorders as interconnected symptom networks, but lacks a biologically grounded, dynamic model of how these networks transition into acute crisis states. This gap limits the identification of critical intervention points.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the acute suicidal state corresponds to a high-dimensional, maladaptive attractor basin in a neural network representing key risk factors, and that graded levels of neural firing rate noise critically influence transitions into and stability within this basin.
proposals.method: We will construct a continuous attractor network model, informed by Rolls' work on cortical attractor dynamics for memory and reward. Key risk factors (anhedonia, threat hypersensitivity, hopelessness) will be modeled as discrete, interacting neuronal populations. Using integrate-and-fire neuronal dynamics with AMPA, NMDA, and GABA synapses, we will systematically vary the level of firing rate noise to simulate how it destabilizes normative states, traps the network in maladaptive short-term memory-like states, and triggers catastrophic transitions to a high-risk attractor. Model parameters will be constrained by pre-2026 neuroimaging and behavioral data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable computational framework linking network psychiatry to underlying cortical and limbic circuit dynamics. It will generate specific predictions for neural noise biomarkers (e.g., from fMRI BOLD variability) and identify computational 'tipping points' that could guide the timing of interventions.
proposals.novelty: It uniquely integrates the conceptual framework of network psychiatry with a biologically detailed, noise-driven attractor network model derived from systems neuroscience, moving beyond static correlation to simulate the dynamic processes of crisis emergence.

proposals.title: Causal Dynamics of Cortical Network Destabilization: Testing a Hippocampo-Cingulate Origin Over Frontal Pole Primacy During Exploratory Scene Processing
proposals.motivation: The frontal pole (FP) is widely implicated in cognitive flexibility and exploration, often cast as a primary driver of network destabilization to facilitate behavioral switching. However, pre-2026 work by Rolls and others highlights the hippocampus's role in generating spatial view and scene representations, and the anterior cingulate cortex's (ACC) role in outcome evaluation and action-outcome learning. This proposal challenges the dominant FP-centric model by hypothesizing that destabilization originates from these earlier, more specialized systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during exploratory visual scene processing, network destabilization is initiated by hippocampal scene representations and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) reward/outcome monitoring, with the frontal pole acting as a follower that integrates and broadcasts this signal rather than generating it de novo.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution 7T fMRI data from humans performing a virtual navigation and exploration task with dynamic reward contingencies. Using a combination of Granger causality analysis and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) on predefined regions (hippocampus, parahippocampal cortex, ACC, FP, orbitofrontal cortex), we will model the directed influence and effective connectivity between nodes. Crucially, we will apply a novel 'perturbation-lagged' analysis, correlating neural activity shifts (destabilization) in each region with subsequent behavioral exploration choices, to establish temporal and causal precedence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a refined, causally-tested model of how exploratory states are neurally initiated, directly challenging a simplistic 'frontal executive' hierarchy. It will integrate Rolls's theories on hippocampal scene representation and ACC motivation with systems-level dynamics, offering a more parsimonious account of exploration grounded in sensory and evaluative processing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the assumed causal hierarchy of frontal control and explicitly testing whether the FP is a consequence, rather than the source, of network changes driven by allocentric scene and motivational systems, a hypothesis not directly addressed in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Non-Reward Inputs to the Frontal Pole: A Computational and Neurobiological Investigation of Novelty-Driven Exploration
proposals.motivation: The orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) and ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) are well-established as key nodes in reward processing, guiding behavior based on expected outcomes. However, the frontal pole (area 10) is implicated in higher-order cognitive functions like exploration and task-switching, yet its inputs are often assumed to be predominantly reward-related. This proposal challenges that exclusivity, investigating whether non-reward signals, particularly novelty detection from systems like the hippocampus and perirhinal cortex, provide critical input to the frontal pole to drive exploratory behavior, a question central to understanding adaptive decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the human frontal pole receives and integrates significant non-reward inputs, specifically novelty and scene-related signals from the hippocampal formation, and that these inputs are computationally sufficient to trigger exploratory behavior in the absence of primary reward prediction error signals.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational modeling and neuroimaging approach. First, a biologically constrained attractor network model of the frontal pole will be extended to include inputs from a hippocampal spatial view cell module (simulating scene novelty) and a perirhinal cortex familiarity module. The model will be tested on virtual navigation tasks where exploration is decoupled from extrinsic reward. Second, we will conduct 7T fMRI in human participants performing similar tasks, using multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode representations of novelty and scene identity in the frontal pole and their functional connectivity with the hippocampus and reward-related OFC/vmPFC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational framework for how non-reward signals gate exploration via the frontal pole, moving beyond a purely reward-centric view of frontal lobe function. It will offer novel empirical evidence for the specific neural pathways linking hippocampal scene/novelty processing to exploratory decision-making in humans, bridging Rolls's theories on scene representation, memory, and reward.
proposals.novelty: While the role of the frontal pole in exploration is recognized, the specific source and nature of its driving inputs remain unclear. This proposal directly tests the non-obvious claim that the frontal pole's exploratory function is triggered not just by low reward, but by dedicated, information-seeking inputs from memory and novelty systems, challenging the primacy of the reward system in this context.

proposals.title: A Gradient-Based Model of Frontal Pole Function: Unifying Reward-Guided Exploitation and Scene-Based Exploration
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroimaging and neurophysiology often parcellate the human frontal pole (Brodmann area 10) into up to six distinct subregions based on structural and functional connectivity. However, this discrete parcellation may obscure a fundamental computational principle. Given Professor Rolls's established theories on reward-guided action in the orbitofrontal cortex and spatial scene representation in the hippocampus, the frontal pole is a critical nexus for integrating these systems to govern high-level behavioral control, particularly the explore-exploit trade-off.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the human frontal pole operates not as a set of discrete modules but as a continuous gradient-based structure, where a principal functional axis organizes neural representations from concrete, reward-value-based exploitation (posterior-ventral) to abstract, scene- or context-based exploration (anterior-dorsal).
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution 7T fMRI data from humans performing a virtual navigation task with explicit explore-exploit blocks. Using Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA), we will test whether neural activity patterns in the frontal pole form a smooth gradient aligned with behavioral states. We will employ a combination of unsupervised manifold learning (e.g., diffusion map embedding) and supervised regression to identify the principal gradient and correlate it with individual differences in exploration strategy. Effective connectivity (Dynamic Causal Modeling) will assess how this gradient modulates interactions with the orbitofrontal cortex (reward) and hippocampus/parahippocampal cortex (scene).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally explicit model of frontal pole function that bridges reward and scene processing systems. It will challenge the dominant discrete parcellation framework by demonstrating a continuous organizational principle directly linked to a core cognitive dilemma. The findings will refine integrative models of frontal lobe function central to understanding disorders of decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While gradients are established in sensory cortex, applying this framework to the frontal pole's high-level control functions is novel. It directly tests and relaxes the strong assumption of functional modularity by linking a macroscopic neural gradient to a specific computational trade-off (explore-exploit) derived from Rolls's prior work.

proposals.title: Dynamic Hippocampal-Cortical and Reward Network Connectivity Underlying Exploitation and Exploration in Scene-Based Decision-Making
proposals.motivation: The neural mechanisms governing the trade-off between exploiting known rewards and exploring novel options are central to adaptive behavior but remain incompletely understood. Pre-2026 work by Rolls and others has established the hippocampus's role in spatial scene representation and memory, and the orbitofrontal cortex's role in reward value, yet how these systems dynamically interact during explicit shifts between exploit and explore states is unknown. This gap limits our computational models of decision-making and our understanding of disorders like addiction or anxiety, where this balance is disrupted.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exploitation of known, high-value scene locations will be characterized by strong, stable connectivity between the hippocampus (spatial view cells) and the orbitofrontal cortex (reward value), while exploration of novel scenes will involve a dynamic reconfiguration, increasing connectivity between the hippocampus and anterior cingulate/ventromedial prefrontal cortex regions involved in monitoring uncertainty and alternative options.
proposals.method: We will use a novel fMRI task where humans navigate virtual scenes, with blocks explicitly cued for 'exploit' (choose previously rewarded locations) or 'explore' (sample novel viewpoints). Dynamic causal modeling (DCM) and time-varying psychophysiological interaction (tvPPI) analyses will be applied to model effective connectivity. Key regions of interest, informed by Rolls's prior work, include the hippocampus (scene), orbitofrontal cortex (reward), anterior cingulate cortex, and ventral visual stream. We will use established pre-2026 toolboxes (SPM12, CONN) for analysis, correlating connectivity parameters with behavioral measures of exploration efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a mechanistic, network-level account of how major brain systems for scene, memory, and reward coordinate during fundamental behavioral states. It will directly test and extend Rolls's theories on hippocampal-cortical interactions by linking them to the exploration-exploitation dilemma. The findings will offer new biomarkers for assessing cognitive flexibility and its breakdown in psychiatric conditions.
proposals.novelty: While exploitation and exploration have been studied in isolation, this proposal uniquely combines Rolls's scene/hippocampus framework with dynamic connectivity analysis to test how *specific* cortical and reward networks reconfigure *during* state transitions, moving beyond static correlations or single-region activation studies.

proposals.title: Integrating Frontal Pole Functional Connectivity with Dopaminergic and Serotonergic Receptor Architectures: A Multi-Modal Framework for Scene and Reward Processing
proposals.motivation: The frontal pole (FP) is a key cortical region implicated in complex cognitive functions, including scene processing, reward evaluation, and memory integration, as highlighted in Rolls's work on spatial view cells and motivation. However, the neurobiological mechanisms underlying its functional diversity remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 advances in multi-modal neuroimaging, such as the JuSpace toolbox, now allow for the spatial correlation of functional connectivity maps with post-mortem neurotransmitter receptor distributions, offering a novel path to link macroscale function with microscale neurochemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional connectivity profile of the human frontal pole, particularly its networks for scene and reward processing, will show significant spatial correspondence with the cortical distributions of dopamine (D1, D2) and serotonin (5-HT1A, 5-HT2A) receptors. This overlap will define distinct neurochemical sub-systems within the FP.
proposals.method: We will use pre-2026 resting-state fMRI data from the Human Connectome Project to generate seed-based functional connectivity maps for the FP. These maps will be spatially correlated, using JuSpace, with high-resolution normative PET templates of dopamine and serotonin receptor densities from the AHEAD atlas. Statistical significance will be assessed via permutation testing. We will then perform a conjunction analysis to identify brain regions where FP connectivity for scene/reward tasks (from meta-analytic data) overlaps with high receptor density zones, using a general linear model framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-modal map linking the FP's functional networks to its underlying receptor architecture, offering a mechanistic explanation for its role in integrating visual, mnemonic, and motivational signals. It will generate testable predictions for how genetic or pharmacological manipulation of these systems affects scene representation and reward-based decision-making, directly extending Rolls's theoretical models.
proposals.novelty: While connectivity and receptor maps have been studied separately, their direct integration to explain the functional specialization of a high-order region like the FP is novel. This moves beyond describing *where* the FP connects to explaining *why* it connects that way, based on a principled neurochemical foundation.

proposals.title: Pharmacological fMRI of Dopaminergic and Serotonergic Modulation on Frontal Pole-Hippocampal Connectivity During Scene-Based Reward Valuation
proposals.motivation: The frontal pole (FP) is a key region for integrating reward, memory, and scene information to guide complex behavior, but its neuromodulatory control remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by Rolls and others has established the role of the hippocampus in spatial scene representation and its connectivity with reward-related frontal areas, yet how specific neurotransmitter systems dynamically regulate these circuits during motivated scene processing is unknown. This gap limits our understanding of how resilience or vulnerability to trauma, which involves altered neuromodulation, might affect these integrative systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dopaminergic (e.g., using a D2/D3 receptor antagonist like amisulpride) and serotonergic (e.g., using a 5-HT1A/2A receptor agonist like psilocybin) pharmacological challenges will differentially modulate functional connectivity between the frontal pole and the hippocampus during a scene-based reward valuation task, reflecting distinct neuromodulatory roles in integrating spatial context with motivational value.
proposals.method: We will employ a double-blind, placebo-controlled, within-subjects pharmacological fMRI design. Participants will perform a validated task where they view complex visual scenes and assign reward values to them, adapted from Rolls's scene representation paradigms. During three separate scanning sessions (placebo, dopaminergic, serotonergic drug), we will acquire resting-state and task-based fMRI. Connectivity will be analyzed using psychophysiological interaction (PPI) and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to assess drug-induced changes in effective connectivity between the FP, hippocampus (posterior), and reward regions (e.g., orbitofrontal cortex, ventral striatum). Pre-2026 standard preprocessing (FSL, SPM) and region-of-interest definitions based on Rolls's anatomical models will be used.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for how specific neuromodulatory systems regulate FP-hippocampal circuits during a core cognitive function. It will directly link Rolls's theoretical models of scene representation and reward to their underlying neurochemical substrates. The findings will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding how genetic or trauma-induced alterations in these systems could affect resilience, by disrupting the integration of context and value.
proposals.novelty: While prior fMRI studies have examined FP connectivity or pharmacological effects separately, this proposal uniquely combines receptor-specific manipulations with a scene-reward integration task grounded in Rolls's precise neurobiological models of the hippocampus and cortex. This moves beyond correlation to test causal neuromodulatory hypotheses about a circuit critical for higher-order cognition.

proposals.title: Frontal Pole Destabilization as a Control Signal for Brain-Wide Network Reconfiguration: A Computational Model Integrating Spatial View, Memory, and Reward Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Rolls and others has established the frontal pole (FP) as a key site for higher-order cognitive control and the hippocampus for spatial scene representation. However, the mechanisms by which a local cognitive 'switch' in the FP triggers a rapid, coordinated reconfiguration of large-scale networks—spanning visual, memory, and reward systems—remain poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to model how cognitive transitions, such as shifting attention or updating goals, are implemented across the whole brain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transient destabilization of activity patterns in the frontal pole serves as a control signal that initiates a cascade of changes in functional connectivity, driving a transition from one whole-brain network state to another, particularly during tasks requiring integration of spatial view information with reward evaluation.
proposals.method: We will develop a large-scale, biologically constrained computational model using dynamic mean-field theory and conductance-based integrate-and-fire neurons. The model will incorporate key regions from Rolls's work: the hippocampus (spatial view cells), orbitofrontal cortex (reward), inferior temporal visual cortex (objects/scenes), and the frontal pole. We will simulate network dynamics using The Virtual Brain (TVB) platform, pre-2026. The FP will be modeled with a metaplasticity rule to induce controlled destabilization. We will analyze resulting whole-brain state transitions using techniques like Leading Eigenvector Dynamics Analysis (LEiDA) and Granger causality on the simulated fMRI and local field potential outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic model of how a local cortical 'switch' orchestrates global brain network transitions, directly linking Rolls's theories on scene representation and reward to systems-level dynamics. It will generate testable predictions for fMRI and electrophysiology on the temporal sequence of reconfiguration across visual, memory, and motivational systems.
proposals.novelty: While network neuroscience describes brain states, this model uniquely proposes a specific, biologically grounded *mechanism* (FP destabilization) for state transitions and integrates it with detailed, region-specific computational neuroscience of spatial, reward, and visual processing as defined by Rolls's pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Testing the Primate-Specificity of Spatial View Representations: A Comparative Electrophysiological Study in Avian and Rodent Species
proposals.motivation: Professor Rolls's work has established that hippocampal spatial view cells in primates encode allocentric, visually defined locations, a representation distinct from rodent place cells. This raises a fundamental question about the evolution of cognitive mapping systems: is this foveal, scene-based representation unique to primates, or does it exist in other highly visual species?
