Resolving this is critical for understanding the link between specialized sensory systems (like high-acuity foveate vision) and the neural mechanisms of episodic memory and spatial navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial view cell-like representations, characterized by allocentric firing to specific viewed locations independent of the animal's own position and orientation, are exclusive to foveate primates and will not be found in non-primate species, even those with excellent vision like birds of prey or tree-dwelling rodents.
proposals.method: We will employ chronic multi-electrode array recordings in the hippocampus and parahippocampal regions of freely moving barn owls (Tyto alba) and gray squirrels (Sciurus carolinensis), chosen for their high visual acuity and ecological reliance on complex 3D spaces. Animals will perform foraging tasks in arboreal-like enclosures with distinct visual landmarks. Neural activity will be correlated with precise eye-tracking (using pre-2026 head-mounted systems) and 3D positional tracking. We will use population vector and Bayesian decoding algorithms (similar to those used in primate studies) to test if viewed location can be decoded independently of the animal's own position and head direction, the hallmark of spatial view cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a major evolutionary hypothesis in cognitive neuroscience, clarifying whether spatial view representations are a primate adaptation or a more general solution for visual navigation. It will expand the comparative framework of hippocampal function beyond the rodent model and inform computational models of scene representation, such as Rolls's VisNet, by defining the necessary sensory inputs for view-based coding.
proposals.novelty: While place cells have been studied across species, no pre-2026 study has systematically searched for primate-like spatial view cells in non-primates using the rigorous combination of free behavior, eye-tracking, and allocentric decoding that defines this cell type. This directly challenges the assumed uniqueness of a core primate cognitive representation.

proposals.title: Linking Transform-Invariant Perirhinal and Inferior Temporal Cortex Face Representations to Hippocampal Spatial View Cells for Episodic Memory Formation
proposals.motivation: The hippocampus forms episodic memories by associating objects like faces with their spatial context, a process central to Rolls' scene representation theory. While inferior temporal cortex (IT) neurons show transform-invariant face recognition, and hippocampal spatial view cells encode location, the specific computational pathway by which invariant object representations are integrated with spatial context to form a coherent memory engram remains unclear. This gap limits our understanding of how the brain builds rich, contextually grounded memories of people.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific subset of perirhinal cortex neurons, receiving convergent inputs from transform-invariant face cells in IT and spatial view cells via the parahippocampal cortex, acts as a critical convergence zone. This convergence enables the hippocampus to bind invariant facial identity to specific spatial contexts during episodic memory encoding.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-stage neurophysiological and computational approach in non-human primates. First, we will record simultaneously from IT face patches, perirhinal cortex, and the hippocampus while subjects view familiar and novel faces at different locations and viewing angles. Second, we will use Granger causality and cross-correlation analysis to identify directed functional connectivity from IT to perirhinal cortex and hippocampus during memory formation tasks. Third, we will implement a hierarchical continuous attractor network model, based on Rolls' prior architectures, to simulate the transformation from invariant object representations in the ventral stream to spatially-tagged memory representations in the hippocampus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct electrophysiological evidence for how invariant object representations are routed to the hippocampus, testing a key prediction of Rolls' theory of scene memory. It will delineate the specific cortical-hippocampal circuit (IT -> perirhinal -> hippocampus) responsible for binding object identity to place, a fundamental operation for episodic memory. The resulting computational model will offer a mechanistic explanation for this binding process.
proposals.novelty: While the roles of IT and hippocampus are well-studied in isolation, the proposal's novelty lies in directly testing the *functional connectivity* between these regions during a memory binding task, specifically focusing on the under-explored perirhinal cortex as a necessary relay for invariant inputs. This moves beyond correlation to identify causal information flow for a defined cognitive operation.

proposals.title: A Large-Scale Computational Model of Episodic Memory Formation Integrating Ventral Visual Stream, Hippocampal Spatial View Cells, and Orbitofrontal Reward Signals
proposals.motivation: Episodic memory formation requires the integration of spatial context, object/face identity, and emotional value, yet existing computational models are limited in scale and biological realism. Building on Rolls' theories of scene representation with hippocampal spatial view cells and the role of the orbitofrontal cortex in reward, a large-scale model is needed to understand how these distributed systems interact during memory encoding and retrieval. This integration is crucial for explaining resilience to trauma, where value signals may modulate memory consolidation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large-scale, biologically constrained neural network model, integrating hierarchical visual processing (V1-IT), hippocampal spatial view and episodic memory systems, and orbitofrontal value representations, will successfully simulate the formation of coherent, value-tagged episodic memories and predict network-level biomarkers of resilience to adverse experiences.
proposals.method: We will construct a multi-regional spiking neural network using the NEST simulator. The architecture will include: 1) a hierarchical ventral visual stream (convolutional attractor networks) for object/face recognition, 2) a hippocampal formation with continuous attractor networks for spatial view cells and a model of episodic memory via pattern separation/completion in the dentate gyrus/CA3, and 3) an orbitofrontal cortex module implementing reward/value associations. These modules will be connected with biologically plausible projections (e.g., perirhinal cortex to hippocampus, hippocampus to orbitofrontal cortex). Learning will employ spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) and reward-modulated STDP.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, systems-level computational model of how spatial scene, identity, and value information converge to form episodic memories. It will generate testable predictions for fMRI and electrophysiological studies on the neural signatures of memory integration. The model will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding how individual differences in these circuits contribute to cognitive-emotional resilience.
proposals.novelty: While models of individual components exist, the novelty lies in the large-scale integration of a detailed ventral visual stream, a spatial view cell-based hippocampal model, and an orbitofrontal value system into a single functioning memory network, simulating the full pathway from sensory input to valued episodic memory.

proposals.title: Semantic Scaffolding of Visual and Reward Representations in the Primate Brain: A Causal Computational Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Rolls and others shows that the brain integrates visual, spatial, and reward information, with semantic knowledge emerging in higher cortical regions like the anterior temporal lobe. However, the dominant model is one of integration, where separate streams converge. The critical, unresolved question is whether semantic representations, once formed, become the primary organizing scaffold that actively drives and constrains subsequent visual and reward processing, rather than merely incorporating their outputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantic representations in high-order association cortices (e.g., anterior temporal lobe, ventromedial prefrontal cortex) are not just endpoints of processing but are causally necessary for the selective, context-dependent integration of visual scene properties and reward valuation observed in regions like the orbitofrontal cortex and hippocampus.
proposals.method: We will combine computational modeling with targeted neurophysiological recordings in non-human primates. A deep neural network (DNN) model with a semantic bottleneck layer will be trained to associate complex visual scenes (containing objects, humans, and spatial layouts) with reward outcomes. We will test if lesioning or perturbing the semantic layer disproportionately disrupts the model's ability to generalize reward associations to novel visual configurations. Concurrently, we will record from hippocampal spatial view cells, orbitofrontal cortex reward cells, and anterior temporal lobe neurons during a scene-reward learning task. Causal tests using reversible inactivation (e.g., muscimol) of the anterior temporal lobe will assess its necessity for maintaining integrated scene-reward representations downstream.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, mechanistic framework for understanding how abstract knowledge guides perception and valuation. It will shift the field's perspective from integration towards a top-down, semantically driven model of cognition. The findings will also inform models of psychiatric disorders where semantic disorganization may underlie aberrant reward processing (e.g., schizophrenia, depression).
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the standard 'bottom-up integration' model to test a 'top-down semantic scaffolding' hypothesis. It moves beyond correlation by employing causal interventions in a biologically constrained computational framework to determine the directionality of influence between semantic, visual, and reward systems.

proposals.title: Developmental Trajectories of the Language Connectome: Effective Connectivity from Childhood to Adulthood and its Relationship to Scene and Reward Processing
proposals.motivation: While the structural and functional connectivity of the language network are well-mapped, the maturation of its *effective connectivity*—the directed, causal influence between regions—remains poorly understood. This is a critical gap, as effective connectivity underpins the dynamic, hierarchical processing required for complex language comprehension and production. Understanding its development is essential for linking brain maturation to cognitive milestones and for identifying biomarkers of neurodevelopmental disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the language effective connectome matures from a more diffuse, feedback-heavy architecture in childhood to a streamlined, feedforward-dominant hierarchy in adulthood. Furthermore, we predict that the strength of effective connectivity from scene-processing regions (e.g., parahippocampal cortex) to language areas will correlate with semantic and narrative abilities, integrating Rolls's work on scene representation with language.
proposals.method: We will analyze a large, longitudinal pediatric neuroimaging dataset (e.g., ABCD Study) using task-based fMRI from a narrative comprehension paradigm. Effective connectivity will be modeled using Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI, a pre-2026 standard, to estimate directed influences between key nodes (inferior frontal gyrus, superior temporal sulcus, angular gyrus). We will implement hierarchical Bayesian modeling of DCM parameters across age to map developmental trajectories. Connectivity strength will be correlated with behavioral scores and, using structural equation modeling, tested for relationships with reward system connectivity, linking to Rolls's interest in motivation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal map of the developing language effective connectome, establishing normative trajectories of causal interaction. It will directly link the maturation of scene-to-language pathways with semantic development, bridging Rolls's spatial view cell theory with cognitive neuroscience. The findings will offer a novel computational framework for understanding how hierarchical processing emerges and could inform models of developmental language disorders.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely applies a causal modeling framework (DCM) to longitudinal developmental data on language, moving beyond correlation. It innovatively tests a specific theoretical integration of scene representation (hippocampal system) and reward systems with the language network's maturation, a synthesis not previously explored in developmental cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: A Whole-Brain Dynamic Model of Scene and Reward Processing: Integrating the Language Connectome into Cortico-Hippocampal Systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Rolls's work, has established distinct roles for the hippocampus in spatial view/scene representation and for cortical regions in reward and language. However, how these large-scale systems interact dynamically to support complex cognition, such as using language to navigate rewarding scenes, remains poorly understood. Embedding the well-mapped human language connectome into a whole-brain dynamic model is a critical next step to move beyond regional analyses and understand integrated cognitive function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the language connectome, particularly its dorsal and ventral streams, acts as a dynamic modulator and integrator, biasing activity in the scene-processing hippocampal system and the reward-processing orbitofrontal cortex during cognitively demanding tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a whole-brain dynamic model using the Human Connectome Project's multimodal parcellation and tractography (pre-2026 data). The model's core will integrate: 1) a hippocampal module implementing spatial view cell dynamics based on Rolls's theoretical framework, 2) an orbitofrontal reward valuation module, and 3) the language connectome (inferior frontal, temporal, parietal regions). We will simulate dynamics using a mean-field or dynamic causal modeling (DCM) framework, testing how perturbations to language network nodes influence information flow and state transitions in the scene-reward system during simulated tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, whole-brain model of how the language network interacts with core systems for scene and reward processing. It will generate testable predictions for functional connectivity and lesion effects, directly linking Rolls's theoretical work on scene representation and emotion to systems-level cognitive neuroscience. The model will serve as a foundational tool for studying resilience and cognitive deficits related to trauma or neurodegeneration.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit integration of a detailed, anatomically grounded language connectome as a control system within a biologically constrained whole-brain model of scene and reward processing, a synthesis not attempted in pre-2026 models which typically treat these systems in isolation.

proposals.title: Integrating Hippocampal Subregion Theory into Whole-Cortex Simulations: A Computational Framework for Scene-Based Memory and Navigation
proposals.motivation: Large-scale brain architecture models, such as whole-cortex simulations, have advanced our understanding of sensory and motor processing but lack detailed, biologically grounded models of the hippocampus and its subregions. Pre-2026, Edmund Rolls's theory of hippocampal spatial view cells and scene representations provides a crucial, yet isolated, account of how the hippocampus supports episodic memory and navigation. Integrating this precise subregional theory into large-scale models is essential to understand how scene and memory systems interact with cortical reward, visual, and reasoning networks to produce unified cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a computational model of hippocampal subregions (CA1, CA3, dentate gyrus) defined by Rolls's spatial view cell theory into a whole-cortex simulation will generate emergent, large-scale dynamics that explain how scene representations guide reward-based navigation and human episodic memory recall.
proposals.method: We will extend a pre-existing large-scale cortical model (e.g., a mean-field or spiking neural network model of macaque or human cortex) by integrating a detailed, multi-layer hippocampal module. This module will implement Rolls's theory, with dentate gyrus performing pattern separation, CA3 auto-associative memory for spatial view scenes, and CA1 forming outputs to reward-related regions like the orbitofrontal cortex. The integration will use known anatomical connectivity (e.g., via parahippocampal and retrosplenial cortices) and be tested in simulated virtual navigation tasks where reward locations are associated with specific scenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale architecture model that explicitly links hippocampal scene representation theory with cortical systems for vision, reward, and memory. It will generate testable predictions for fMRI and electrophysiology on how hippocampal-cortical interactions fail in conditions like trauma or amnesia. The resulting open-source framework will be a new benchmark for simulating integrated cognitive functions.
proposals.novelty: While large-scale models and hippocampal theories exist separately, their integration is non-trivial and has not been systematically attempted. The novelty lies in scaling Rolls's specific subregional theory of scene representation to a full cortical architecture, moving beyond isolated hippocampal models to a system-level account of cognition.

proposals.title: Inferring Hippocampal Subfield Dysfunction from Behavioral Profiles: A Computational Lesion Mapping Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Rolls' work on spatial view cells, establishes that distinct hippocampal subregions (e.g., CA1, CA3, dentate gyrus) support specialized functions in scene memory, navigation, and reward association. However, linking specific behavioral deficits to dysfunction in a particular subregion remains a clinical and theoretical challenge. A method to infer subregion-level pathology from behavior would advance precision neuropsychiatry and test computational theories of hippocampal circuitry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multivariate pattern of behavioral performance on tasks probing scene memory, spatial navigation, and reward-context learning can be used to accurately predict the specific hippocampal subfield (CA1, CA3, DG) that is functionally compromised.
proposals.method: We will develop a machine learning pipeline using pre-2026 methods. First, we will simulate 'virtual lesions' in a pre-existing computational model of the hippocampal circuit (e.g., a continuous attractor network for spatial view cells). The model will generate synthetic behavioral profiles for tasks sensitive to CA1, CA3, or DG function. These synthetic behavioral vectors will train a classifier (e.g., a Support Vector Machine or Random Forest) to map behavior to subregion. We will then validate the algorithm on real behavioral and high-resolution 7T MRI data from cohorts with known hippocampal pathology (e.g., early Alzheimer's, TLE) where subfield atrophy is measured.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational tool for non-invasive 'lesion inference,' moving beyond gross hippocampal volume to subfield-specific dysfunction. It will provide a stronger empirical test of computational theories of subregional specialization. The method could stratify patient populations for targeted interventions based on inferred circuit pathology.
proposals.novelty: While forward models predict behavior from lesions, the inverse problem—inferring lesion location from behavior—is rarely tackled for hippocampal subfields. Combining Rolls' theoretical models of scene representation with machine learning for diagnostic inference is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Direct Anatomical and Functional Connectivity from the Superior Temporal Sulcus 'What' Stream to the Orbitofrontal Cortex: A Primate Neuroimaging and Tract-Tracing Study
proposals.motivation: The primate superior temporal sulcus (STS) is a key node in the visual 'What' stream for processing social cues like faces and biological motion, while the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) is critical for emotion and social behavior. A major theoretical proposal, consistent with Rolls's work on visual-to-emotion pathways, is that the STS provides high-level social perceptual input to the OFC, but this specific anatomical connectivity remains poorly defined and empirically untested in primates. Establishing this link is fundamental to understanding how visual social perception guides motivated behavior and emotion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific subregions within the anterior and middle STS, which are responsive to social stimuli, possess direct, monosynaptic anatomical projections to discrete subregions of the OFC, and that the strength of this structural connectivity will correlate with functional coupling during the viewing of socially-relevant visual scenes.
proposals.method: We will combine high-resolution diffusion-weighted MRI (dMRI) tractography and resting-state fMRI in awake, behaving macaques (N=6) to map STS-OFC pathways in vivo. This will be followed by definitive anatomical validation using anterograde and retrograde neuronal tract-tracing injections (e.g., biotinylated dextran amine) placed in fMRI-defined STS sites, with histological analysis. Functional connectivity will be assessed during passive viewing of social (human faces, interactions) versus non-social scenes, using generalized psychophysiological interaction (gPPI) analysis. All analyses will use established, pre-2026 toolboxes (FSL, MRtrix3, SPM).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct empirical evidence for or against a monosynaptic pathway from the STS 'What' stream to the OFC, a cornerstone of models linking social perception to emotion. It will delineate the precise topographic organization of this pathway, offering a structural basis for how specific social cues gain affective value. The data will critically constrain computational models of social decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While connectivity of the ventral visual stream to amygdala is established, the proposed direct STS-to-OFC projection is a specific, anatomically-precise hypothesis that has not been systematically tested. The combination of state-of-the-art in vivo neuroimaging with gold-standard tract-tracing for validation is a powerful, non-obvious approach to bridge systems-level and microcircuit understanding.

proposals.title: Mapping Egocentric-to-Allocentric Coordinate Transforms from Dorsal Parietal Cortex to the Parahippocampal Scene Area for Self-Motion Updating
proposals.motivation: The parahippocampal scene area (PPA) and hippocampus are critical for representing allocentric scenes and spatial view, but the computational origin of these representations remains unclear. Pre-2026 work by Rolls and others shows that hippocampal spatial view cells require self-motion updates, yet the specific cortical pathway transforming egocentric dorsal stream signals (e.g., from VIP, 7a) into the allocentric scene inputs for the PPA is not mapped. This gap prevents a systems-level understanding of how visual and self-motion cues build scene memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific subregions within the dorsal visual stream (particularly areas VIP and 7a) perform a sequential, hierarchical coordinate transformation from egocentric, body-centered motion signals to the allocentric, world-centered scene representations found in the PPA.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution fMRI in humans performing a virtual navigation task with controlled visual and vestibular self-motion cues. Using psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analysis and dynamic causal modeling (DCM), we will map effective connectivity from dorsal parietal areas (VIP, 7a) to the PPA. Concurrently, we will develop a hierarchical, biologically-plausible neural network model of the dorsal stream, implementing continuous attractor networks to perform the egocentric-to-allocentric transform, and fit its activity patterns to the fMRI data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional and computational map of the coordinate transform pathway feeding the PPA, directly linking Rolls's theory of hippocampal scene representation to its cortical inputs. It will identify the specific parietal computations for self-motion updating, a key missing link in models of spatial memory and navigation.
proposals.novelty: While coordinate transforms are theorized, this proposal uniquely seeks to empirically identify and model the specific *cortical hierarchy* implementing this transform for *scene representation*, bridging the dorsal 'where' stream with the ventral 'what' stream's scene-processing regions in a way not previously tested.

proposals.title: Dual Parieto-Retrosplenial 'Where' Streams in the Rodent: A Homologous Circuit for Scene and Self-Motion Processing
proposals.motivation: Primate research, including work by Rolls and colleagues, identifies a dual-stream 'Where' system for spatial scene and navigation processing, involving dorsal visual stream projections to parietal cortex and a separate pathway via retrosplenial cortex. It is unknown whether this fundamental cortical organization is a primate specialization or a conserved mammalian feature. Establishing a homologous system in the rodent model is crucial for leveraging powerful genetic, circuit-level, and behavioral tools to mechanistically dissect the neural basis of spatial cognition and its disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rodent brain contains homologous, functionally distinct 'Where' streams: a parietal-dominant stream for egocentric spatial localization and a retrosplenial-dominant stream for allocentric scene representation and self-motion integration, analogous to the primate system.
proposals.method: We will use high-density Neuropixels probes and two-photon calcium imaging in head-fixed mice navigating virtual reality environments. We will record simultaneously from posterior parietal cortex (PPC) and retrosplenial cortex (RSC) during tasks dissociating egocentric cue-response from allocentric scene-based navigation. Neural population analyses (linear classifiers, demixed principal component analysis) will quantify the distinct coding of egocentric bearing, allocentric scene features, and self-motion. Anatomical connectivity will be verified using retrograde viral tracing from PPC and RSC to visual cortical areas.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational rodent model for the dual 'Where' streams, bridging primate systems neuroscience with rodent circuit-level interrogation. It will provide a concrete circuit framework for understanding how distinct spatial representations are computed and integrated, with direct relevance to models of hippocampal-parietal-retrosplenial interactions in memory and navigation.
proposals.novelty: While rodent PPC and RSC are studied separately, the explicit test for a homologous, parallel organization mirroring the primate dual-stream model is novel. It moves beyond cataloging regional functions to defining a conserved mammalian cortical systems architecture for spatial processing.

proposals.title: Top-Down Modulation of Visual Streams by Episodic and Semantic Memory Content: A Computational and fMRI Investigation
proposals.motivation: The dominant model of visual processing describes a largely feedforward hierarchy. However, pre-2026 work by Rolls and others highlights the role of memory systems like the hippocampus in constructing scene representations. The critical, unresolved question is how stored episodic and semantic memories retroactively and dynamically shape ongoing processing in the dorsal (action) and ventral (perception) visual streams, a process fundamental to perception, navigation, and resilience to trauma.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that reactivated episodic and semantic memory content will exert a selective, content-specific top-down modulation on visual stream processing, with hippocampal-cortical pathways differentially biasing the dorsal stream for spatial-action content and the ventral stream for object-scene content.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined fMRI and computational modeling approach. Participants will first encode complex scenes paired with semantic labels. During a subsequent fMRI session, they will perform a degraded visual perception task where ambiguous stimuli are preceded by cues triggering the retrieval of associated episodic or semantic content. We will use Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) to quantify how memory reactivation alters multivariate activity patterns in visual stream regions (V1-V4, PPA, RSC for ventral; MT+, parietal regions for dorsal). A biologically constrained hierarchical attractor network model, based on Rolls's prior architectures, will simulate the interaction between hippocampal/parietal memory networks and visual cortical areas to predict the observed modulation patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic account of how memory systems actively sculpt sensory processing, moving beyond correlational descriptions. It will directly test and extend Rolls's theories on scene representation and hippocampal function by specifying their top-down influence on visual streams. The findings will offer a novel framework for understanding how prior knowledge and experience, including traumatic memories, can alter perceptual reality.
proposals.novelty: While feedback is acknowledged, this proposal uniquely focuses on how specific *memory content* (episodic vs. semantic) retroactively modulates distinct visual streams, a causal and content-specific hypothesis not directly tested in pre-2026 literature. It integrates Rolls's work on scene cells, memory, and reward into a unified top-down model of perception.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Scene and Reward Representation in the Ventral Visual Stream: A Computational Goal in Parahippocampal Cortex
proposals.motivation: The ventral visual stream's role in object and scene processing is well-established, with a traditional view that its primary endpoint is the hippocampus for memory formation. However, pre-2026 work by Rolls and others shows that parahippocampal regions like the parahippocampal place area (PPA) and retrosplenial cortex (RSC) are crucial for scene representation and spatial view. This suggests core computations for scene-based reward and memory may be completed earlier, challenging the hippocampus's role as the sole endpoint.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary computational goal of the ventral visual stream for scene processing is the formation of integrated scene-reward representations in parahippocampal cortex, not merely the relay of information to the hippocampus for episodic memory.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution 7T fMRI data from human participants viewing complex scenes with varying reward associations. Using representational similarity analysis (RSA), we will compare neural representational geometries across ventral stream (e.g., V1, V4), object-selective (LOC), and parahippocampal (PPA, RSC) regions. We will employ computational models, including deep convolutional neural networks (CNNs) trained on scene categorization and a reinforcement learning model of reward prediction, to test whether model layer activations best correlate with parahippocampal activity patterns during scene-reward learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, computational account of where in the cortical hierarchy the integration of visual scene information with reward value is achieved. It will clarify the functional specialization of parahippocampal regions versus the hippocampus, directly testing theories of scene representation and reward processing proposed by Rolls and others.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges the canonical view of the hippocampus as the primary endpoint by positing that a core computational goal (scene-reward integration) is met earlier. It integrates Rolls's theories on scene representation (spatial view cells) and reward with modern multivariate fMRI and deep learning methods in a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Developmental Trajectory of the Ventromedial Visual Stream to the Hippocampus: A Longitudinal fMRI Study from Childhood to Adulthood
proposals.motivation: The ventromedial visual stream, projecting from the inferior temporal visual cortex to the hippocampus, is critical for scene perception, spatial memory, and reward-related processing. Pre-2026 work, including Rolls's model of hippocampal spatial view cells, posits this pathway as foundational for scene representation. However, its developmental timeline remains poorly characterized, limiting our understanding of how key cognitive capacities like episodic memory and spatial navigation mature.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional connectivity and representational specificity within the ventromedial visual stream-hippocampus pathway will show a protracted, non-linear developmental trajectory, with significant reorganization during adolescence correlating with improvements in scene memory and reward-based spatial learning.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal fMRI study in participants aged 8-25, with scans at 2-year intervals. Using pre-2026 methods, we will employ a multi-faceted paradigm: 1) Scene vs. object localizer tasks to map category-selectivity in visual cortical regions (e.g., parahippocampal place area), 2) A spatial view-based memory task to engage the hippocampus, and 3) A reward-conditioned navigation task. Functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) and multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) will be used to assess developmental changes in pathway integration and information content. Genetic and trauma-exposure data will be included as covariates, building on Rolls's recent work on resilience markers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of the development of a key cortical-hippocampal pathway for scene and reward processing. It will link normative neurodevelopmental changes to the maturation of specific memory and navigation behaviors. Furthermore, it will establish a baseline against which atypical development, potentially influenced by genetic risk or early trauma, can be understood.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined the development of isolated visual or memory regions, this proposal uniquely tracks the maturation of their functional integration along a defined anatomical pathway. It directly tests a developmental component of Rolls's theoretical model of scene representation, integrating it with the study of reward, a core theme of his work.

proposals.title: A Primate-Centric Computational Model of Scene Representation: Building AI Spatial Systems from Hippocampal View Cells
proposals.motivation: Current AI models of spatial cognition and navigation are overwhelmingly derived from rodent place cell data, which encode an animal's allocentric location. However, primate navigation, scene understanding, and memory are fundamentally guided by hippocampal spatial view cells, which encode allocentric locations in the environment *from a specific vantage point*. This primate-centric, viewpoint-dependent representation is likely more relevant for developing AI that understands complex scenes and navigates in human-like ways, yet it remains underexplored in computational neuroscience and AI.
proposals.hypothesis: That an artificial neural network model, trained to predict the next view in a sequence and grounded in the known properties of primate hippocampal spatial view cells and primate ventral visual stream processing, will generate more human-like scene representations and support more efficient view-based navigation than models derived from rodent place cell paradigms.
proposals.method: We will construct a deep learning architecture with a hierarchical convolutional pathway (simulating primate ventral visual stream, V1 to IT) feeding into a recurrent network module (simulating parahippocampal and hippocampal circuits). This network will be trained on large-scale datasets of egocentric video sequences from virtual primate environments. The core objective is a predictive coding task: given a sequence of views, the model must learn to activate a stable population code (the 'view cell' layer) for specific allocentric locations in the visual scene, independent of the agent's exact orientation at that location, and predict the next likely view. We will compare the emergent representations to known electrophysiological data from non-human primates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first major AI model of spatial processing explicitly built on primate neurobiology, shifting the paradigm from location-centric to view-centric representation. It will generate testable predictions for primate hippocampal function and offer a novel architecture for AI scene understanding, with potential applications in robotics and virtual agent navigation where viewpoint is primary.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the dominant rodent-to-primate modeling progression by starting with the primate neural data (spatial view cells) as the foundational computational primitive, rather than treating primate mechanisms as a complex extension of a rodent model.

proposals.title: A Unified Cortical-Hippocampal Model of Memory Retrieval and Generative Scene Prediction
proposals.motivation: Traditional computational models of memory and scene understanding often treat episodic memory retrieval and generative prediction as separate systems. This separation is at odds with the integrated, continuous nature of cognition and with Rolls's theory of hippocampal spatial view cells as a foundation for scene representation. A unified framework is needed to explain how the same neural circuits support both recalling past experiences and constructing plausible future or alternative scenes, a core function for navigation and planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primate ventral visual stream, interacting with the hippocampus and reward-related orbitofrontal cortex, implements a single, continuous attractor network process where memory recall and generative scene completion/prediction are emergent properties of the same dynamical system, modulated by reward context.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical, continuous attractor network model grounded in primate neuroanatomy. The model will integrate: 1) A ventral visual stream hierarchy (V1-IT) forming invariant object representations, 2) A hippocampal CA3 recurrent network storing spatial view cell representations of scenes, and 3) An orbitofrontal cortex module providing reward-modulated bias. Generative prediction will be modeled as pattern completion in the CA3 attractor network from partial or noisy inputs, with the system's trajectory influenced by reward state to simulate goal-directed scene construction. We will test the model's ability to both retrieve stored scene memories and generate coherent novel scene views using pre-2026 datasets of spatial view cell firing and human fMRI data on scene perception.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable computational model that bridges Rolls's theories of scene representation with modern predictive processing frameworks. It will offer a mechanistic explanation for how memory and imagination share a common neural substrate in the hippocampal-cortical system. The model will generate specific, falsifiable predictions for neural activity during memory retrieval versus future scene simulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling memory and generation as a single, reward-modulated attractor process within a biologically constrained primate cortical-hippocampal architecture, moving beyond separate 'retrieval' and 'generation' modules commonly used in AI or rodent-focused models.

proposals.title: A Neurobiologically-Inspired Transformer: Integrating Primate Spatial View Cell Computations for Scene-Consistent Generative AI
proposals.motivation: Current generative AI models, such as diffusion models and autoregressive transformers, lack a persistent, allocentric representation of space, leading to inconsistencies in object location and viewpoint across generated scene sequences. Pre-2026 neuroscience, particularly the work of Rolls and colleagues, has established that primate hippocampal spatial view cells and parietal mechanisms provide a stable, viewpoint-invariant representation of object locations within scenes, which is fundamental for navigation and memory. Integrating these principles could address a core limitation in AI's scene understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a transformer-based generative architecture with a computational module that mimics the function of primate spatial view cells will significantly improve the spatial consistency and coherence of generated multi-view scene sequences compared to standard models.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel 'Spatial View Transformer' (SVT) architecture. A standard vision transformer (ViT) encoder will extract object-centric features from input images. These features will be fed into a dedicated 'Allocentric Map Module'—a persistent, latent 2D map that learns to bind object identity to allocentric (world-centered) coordinates, analogous to spatial view cell representations. During generation, a transformer decoder will attend to both the learned object features and their dynamically queried locations from this allocentric map, conditioned on a desired egocentric viewpoint. Training will use datasets of sequential scene views (e.g., Matterport3D) with paired egocentric images and allocentric coordinate metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete computational model linking a well-established neurobiological theory of scene representation to state-of-the-art AI. It will deliver a novel generative architecture capable of producing more spatially coherent scenes, advancing the field of neuro-symbolic AI. The project will also generate testable predictions for neuroscience regarding how coordinate transforms between egocentric and allocentric frames may be implemented in cortical-hippocampal circuits.
proposals.novelty: While prior AI work has used graph networks or slot attention for objects, explicitly grounding object representations in a persistent, allocentric 2D coordinate map inspired by primate spatial view cells is a novel approach. Furthermore, integrating this neurobiological mechanism directly into a generative transformer framework for controllable scene synthesis is unexplored.

proposals.title: Network Dysregulation of Visceral-Affective Integration in Irritable Bowel Syndrome: A Graph-Theoretic Analysis of Resting-State and Reward-Related fMRI
proposals.motivation: Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) is a prevalent gut-brain axis disorder characterized by chronic abdominal pain and altered emotional states, but its neural substrates remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research implicates central networks like the salience, default mode, and reward systems, yet a formal, systems-level characterization of IBS as a dysregulated brain network is lacking. Applying Edmund Rolls's frameworks for reward, emotion, and scene representation to this visceral domain offers a principled way to model how gut signals are integrated into affective and cognitive brain states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IBS patients exhibit a specific, quantifiable dysregulation in the topological properties of large-scale brain networks, characterized by altered connectivity between visceral-sensory (e.g., insula), affective (e.g., amygdala, orbitofrontal cortex), and scene/context-representation (e.g., hippocampus, parahippocampal cortex) regions, particularly during reward anticipation and resting state.
proposals.method: We will acquire resting-state and task-based fMRI (using a monetary incentive delay task) from IBS patients and matched controls. Using preprocessed data, we will construct whole-brain functional connectivity matrices. Graph theory metrics (e.g., modularity, global/local efficiency, hub identification via betweenness centrality) will be computed using established toolboxes (e.g., Brain Connectivity Toolbox). We will specifically test for altered connectivity within and between pre-defined networks (salience, default mode, central executive) and Rolls-relevant regions (orbitofrontal cortex for reward, hippocampus for context). Statistical comparisons will use network-based statistic (NBS) and regression against clinical symptom severity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal graph-theoretic model of the IBS brain, moving beyond regional activations to a systems-level understanding of dysregulation. It will directly link Rolls's theories of reward and hippocampal scene representation to the pathophysiology of a visceral disorder, identifying potential network biomarkers of symptom severity. The findings could inform future neuromodulation targets by pinpointing critical dysregulated hubs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Rolls's integrative framework of reward, emotion, and spatial-context representation—developed largely in visual and memory domains—to model network dysfunction in a visceral pain disorder. Combining graph theory with a specific reward/affective task to probe the gut-brain axis represents a significant methodological and conceptual advance over standard resting-state analyses in IBS.

ideas.title: Modeling Suicide Risk as a Noisy Attractor Network: A Computational Network Psychiatry Approach
ideas.description: This research synthesizes network medicine and computational neuroscience by modeling the interconnected risk factors for suicide (e.g., anhedonia, impulsivity, sleep disturbance) as a high-dimensional attractor network. The core innovation is to examine how biologically plausible, graded levels of firing rate noise within the neural representations of these factors influence the network's dynamics—specifically, the detection of threat signals, the maintenance of maladaptive cognitive states in short-term memory, and catastrophic transitions into high-risk attractor basins. This approach moves beyond mapping static correlations to simulating the dynamic, noise-driven processes that may tip an individual's psychological system from a vulnerable state into an acute crisis, offering testable predictions for biomarkers and intervention timing.

ideas.title: Challenge the independence of high-dimensional neural and phenotypic noise sources
ideas.description: This research direction proposes that the dominant sources of noise in large-scale cortical network activity are not independent, intrinsic neural processes but are instead systematically structured by the high-dimensional phenome of the organism. We will model the 2,291 phenotypic factors and their complex interactions to predict the covariance structure of multi-region neural variability, moving beyond the graded vs. spiking noise dichotomy. The core hypothesis is that collinear phenotypic traits (e.g., linked metabolic, anatomical, and behavioral measures) generate correlated constraints on network states, creating a low-dimensional manifold of dominant shared noise. This tests whether the statistical architecture of an organism's phenome is the primary determinant of its brain's collective variability patterns.

ideas.title: Generative Tiling of Canonical Graded-Attractor Microcircuits for Whole-Cortex Modeling
ideas.description: This project establishes a rigorous, generative framework for scaling biologically grounded cortical models. It begins by formally defining a canonical, graded attractor microcircuit as the fundamental computational unit, whose dynamics are validated against in-vivo cortical recordings. A generative tiling engine then assembles a whole-cortex model by instantiating and connecting thousands of these units, strictly following rules derived from empirical data on inter-areal connectivity, topographic mapping, and hierarchical organization. The resulting model is not merely a scaled-up fragment but a systematically constructed, large-scale network whose architecture and emergent large-scale dynamics are direct, testable consequences of biological first principles.

ideas.title: Demographic-Aware Comparison of Graded vs. Binary Neural Representations Under Natural Distributional Constraints
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to test if the relative efficiency and causal explanatory power of graded versus sparse binary neural representations differ across demographic strata (e.g., age, sex, socioeconomic status). Instead of artificially matching sparseness levels, it allows the natural distribution parameters of each group's neural data to guide the comparison, examining whether optimal coding schemes are population-specific. This reveals if core computational constraints in models of brain function are fixed or are shaped by developmental, experiential, or biological factors varying across demographics. The synergy lies in using demographic heterogeneity as a natural experiment to stress-test the generality of fundamental representational theories in computational neuroscience.

ideas.title: Causal Optimization of Neural Representations for Speed-Stability Trade-offs
ideas.description: This research direction integrates causal discovery algorithms with the optimization of neural firing rate distributions for decision speed and stability. We apply constraint-based causal discovery to high-dimensional neural recordings during decision-making tasks to identify which neural populations or connectivity patterns causally influence the speed-stability trade-off frontier. The discovered causal graph then directly constrains and informs the optimization process, allowing us to search for optimal representations not just in abstract parameter space, but within the subspace of causally plausible network configurations. This yields testable hypotheses about which specific circuit interventions (e.g., modulating a particular inhibitory pathway) should most efficiently shift the system towards a desired point on the speed-accuracy trade-off curve.

ideas.description: This research fuses the search for inverted causality in behavioral resilience with the scaling of core neural dynamics. It proposes a large-scale computational experiment where synaptic time constants and firing rate distributions are systematically varied as simulated 'liability factors' in neural circuit models of psychiatric risk (e.g., for schizophrenia). The key innovation is to identify parameter regimes where increasing a putative liability (e.g., longer synaptic decay) paradoxically reduces network-level noise or instability metrics, thereby revealing a causal, mechanistic signature of resilience. This approach moves beyond statistical association in human data to directly test and discover which dynamical aspect—synaptic integration or firing distribution—dominates in generating protective effects against pathological neural noise.

ideas.title: Dynamic Gain Control for Context-Dependent Speed-Accuracy Trade-offs
ideas.description: This project investigates how neural populations should dynamically reconfigure their collective gain and noise structure to optimally balance decision speed and stability under changing conditions. Instead of a fixed optimal firing rate distribution, we model a control system that uses estimates of environmental volatility and task urgency to adjust population-level parameters in real time. The core advance is a normative theory for adaptive neural coding that specifies how such a dynamic policy should be implemented through neuromodulation or recurrent circuit mechanisms to maximize cumulative reward across diverse contexts.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Dynamic Neural Representational States
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to intensively sample behavioral, subjective, and psychophysiological data from individuals as they perform tasks in real-world settings. The core aim is to detect and validate signatures of the brain's proposed dynamic switching between graded (fine-grained, analog) and binary (discrete, categorical) representational states. By analyzing high-frequency, multi-modal EMA data, we can test predictions about when and why such switches occur—linking them to task demands, cognitive load, and environmental context—thereby grounding a computational theory in empirically observable human experience.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Noise Shaping for Adaptive Neural Representations
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a unified control-theoretic framework to investigate the co-evolution of neural representations and network noise. Instead of merely stabilizing a spontaneous state against noise, the framework designs feedback controllers that actively shape the statistical properties of intrinsic noise to bias learning towards either graded or discrete representations based on task demands. This allows the system to adaptively harness noise as a computational resource, for instance, using shaped noise to encourage exploratory, graded coding during learning and stable, binary coding for robust recall. The core synergy is treating noise not as a disturbance to be rejected, but as a manipulable control signal that governs the representational geometry of the network itself.

ideas.title: Demographic-Stratified Information Geometry of Neural Firing for Causal Inference
ideas.description: This research direction integrates demographic stratification with information-geometric analysis of neural firing models. It proposes to test whether the Fisher information metric or other geometric properties (e.g., curvature of the statistical manifold) of simulated or recorded neural firing distributions differ significantly across age, sex, or socioeconomic strata. This allows for a rigorous, geometry-based test of the uniformity assumption in computational neuroscience models, revealing if certain demographic groups operate with inherently different neural coding efficiency or noise characteristics that could bias causal inferences drawn from pooled data.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Representation Type from Sparseness in Neural Comparisons
ideas.description: This approach formally separates the causal effect of using graded versus binary representations from the confounding effect of their differing sparseness distributions. It uses techniques like propensity score matching or covariate adjustment to create fair comparisons across representation types while allowing their natural sparseness parameters. The method includes a rigorous sensitivity analysis to report how strongly an unmeasured confounder would need to act to invalidate the primary conclusion. This yields interpretable, robust findings about the intrinsic advantages or disadvantages of each representation scheme.

ideas.title: Decoupling Intrinsic and Extrinsic Noise in Neural Dynamics via Dimensional Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from chemical reaction engineering, where dimensional analysis is used to separate intrinsic molecular noise from extrinsic environmental fluctuations. The analogous problem is determining which source dominates variability in a system's output. Here, we apply this to neuroscience by constructing a dimensionless noise dominance parameter (NDP) for a neural population. The NDP scales the characteristic time of synaptic integration (synaptic time constants) and transmission (mean firing rate) against the magnitude and correlation time of fluctuations in the presynaptic firing distribution. By systematically varying synaptic constants and firing distribution parameters (e.g., mean, variance, autocorrelation) in simulations or mean-field models, we can generate a phase diagram. This diagram will show the regions where internal synaptic dynamics primarily shape output noise versus where the statistics of the external firing distribution are the dominant factor, effectively decoupling the two influences proposed in the original seed idea.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery of Noise Regimes Using Gamma vs. Exponential Distributional Signatures
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the systematic comparison of gamma and exponential firing rate distributions directly into the pipeline of constraint-based causal discovery algorithms. The core method involves using the distinct statistical signatures of these distributions—where gamma can model multi-stage processes and exponential pure Poisson-like randomness—as conditional independence tests or as assumptions within the causal model to disambiguate between different latent noise regimes. This allows the algorithm to propose causal graphs where the edges are not only directed but also annotated with a hypothesized generative noise model (e.g., 'gamma-driven' vs. 'exponential-driven'), suggesting fundamentally different underlying biophysical or circuit mechanisms. Consequently, the fusion generates novel, testable causal hypotheses that are explicitly tied to the statistical physics of neural activity, going beyond topology to infer the qualitative nature of influences.

ideas.title: Noise-Driven Resilience: Quantifying Stochastic Advantages in Protective Behavioral Phenotypes
ideas.description: This research program fuses the formal characterization of noise benefits for reaction times with the search for inverted causality in behavioral resilience. It hypothesizes that certain protective factors against maladaptive outcomes (e.g., substance use) may operate by strategically harnessing increased system noise. The core aim is to develop computational models and experimental paradigms to identify phenotypes where higher liability (e.g., genetic risk, trait impulsivity) is linked to faster, noise-advantaged decisions *away* from risk cues, effectively quantifying a 'stochastic shield' mechanism. This moves beyond correlational protection to a mechanistic understanding of how noise can be leveraged for adaptive behavioral control.

ideas.title: Information-geometric optimization of neural codes for speed-stability trade-offs
ideas.description: This research fuses information geometry with dynamical optimization to solve for the neural firing distribution that optimally balances decision speed against stability. It uses the Fisher information metric from Idea A to quantify the noise and discriminability inherent to different distributional families (binary, exponential, etc.) within a neural population. This geometric characterization is then integrated into the speed-stability trade-off framework from Idea B, allowing the derivation of a theoretically optimal code that maximizes the rate of reliable information extraction for decision-making under biological constraints. The result is a principled theory predicting when sparse, bursty, or regular firing patterns are optimal for rapid and stable perceptual decisions.

ideas.title: Adapting Stochastic Resonance from Sensory Biology to Shape Neural Representations
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of stochastic resonance from sensory neurobiology to the study of how inherent network noise shapes representation development. In biology, stochastic resonance shows that an optimal amount of noise can enhance signal detection in sensory neurons. The analogous solution is to intentionally inject and modulate noise during neural network training, rather than merely suppressing it. The adapted idea is to conduct a series of scaling experiments where the type and amplitude of noise (e.g., Gaussian, dropout rate, quantization noise) is a controlled variable. The goal is to map the 'noise landscape' to determine the conditions under which noise promotes rich, graded representations versus forcing a shift to binary, noise-invariant codes. This tests the inverse relationship between noise and representation fidelity within the SCALING_DUAL dimension, potentially leading to noise-informed training regimes for more efficient and robust networks.

ideas.title: Fokker-Planck Control of Dynamical Representation Gradation in Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research direction models a neural network's active regulation of representation gradation as a stochastic dynamical system, where the network state diffuses in a potential landscape shaped by task demands. The Fokker-Planck formalism is used to analytically compute the escape times from metastable graded or binary attractor states. By inverting this relationship, the network can dynamically adjust its effective noise or coupling parameters to control these escape times, thereby strategically switching representation modes to optimize for speed, accuracy, or energy efficiency based on real-time performance targets. This creates a principled, physics-inspired framework for understanding and designing adaptive neural computation.

ideas.title: A Multi-Level Framework to Disentangle and Validate Noise-Driven Speedups in Decision Circuits
ideas.description: This research establishes a rigorous framework to dissect when and how internal noise can accelerate decision-making, moving beyond a generic characterization. It systematically tests the effects of distinct biological and computational noise types across a hierarchy of models, from abstract to biophysically detailed. The core innovation is an information-theoretic 'noise titration' protocol that separates robust stochastic resonance effects from model-specific artifacts. The outcome will be a set of falsifiable, experimentally grounded principles predicting the specific neural signatures and task conditions where a noise advantage for speed should—and should not—be observable.

ideas.title: Embedded Micro-Intervention EMA for Causal Pathway Validation
ideas.description: This redesigned system combines intensive longitudinal Ecological Momentary Assessment with embedded, randomized micro-interventions to move beyond correlation and test causal mechanisms. By randomly prompting participants to engage in specific, brief behaviors or cognitive exercises at critical moments identified via EMA, the system can observe differential subsequent outcomes. This allows for the direct validation of whether a hypothesized behavioral or cognitive risk factor is merely associated with an outcome or plays a causal role in its real-world occurrence. The approach thus provides robust, ecologically valid evidence for causal behavioral pathways.

ideas.title: Synaptic and Representational Plasticity for Noise-Adaptive Computation
ideas.description: This research investigates how neural networks can co-adapt their synaptic dynamics and firing distribution regimes to actively manage noise and optimize computational performance. We propose that networks do not merely switch between graded and binary representations, but that this switch actively governs whether synaptic interactions operate in a fast, integration-leaky regime (suited for precise, noisy graded signals) or a slow, integration-persistent regime (suited for stable, binary decisions). By modeling this coupled system, we can discover how the representational state instructs the effective synaptic time scale, and vice-versa, to create noise-robust computations. This allows us to identify fundamental principles for how biological or artificial systems might dynamically tune their own computational 'material properties' in real-time.

ideas.title: Causal Intervention Framework for Network Noise and Representational Format
ideas.description: This research implements a system to causally test how inherent noise shapes neural representations by building a framework for precise, in-training manipulation of noise sources within artificial neural networks. It employs techniques like targeted gradient noise injection, activation jitter with controlled power spectra, and noise-aware regularization to actively sculpt the network's internal stochastic environment. The core experiments compare the emergent representational formats (graded vs. binary) across identical architectures and tasks under systematically varied noise regimes, isolating noise as the independent variable. This approach transforms the study from a correlational observation into a causal engineering testbed, directly revealing if and how specific noise properties can be harnessed to bias representational development.

ideas.title: Demographic-Specific Neural Noise and the Development of Risk Representation Profiles
ideas.description: This research investigates how the inherent noise present in artificial neural networks interacts with demographic-specific data distributions to shape the development of internal representations for risk prediction. It tests the hypothesis that differences in model accuracy and identified causal factors across age, sex, or socioeconomic strata emerge because network noise is shaped differently during training on each subgroup, leading to distinct representational profiles (e.g., more graded versus more binary coding schemes). The study would involve training models on stratified data and analyzing both performance metrics and the noise-shaping dynamics within each demographic-specific sub-model. The goal is to determine if disparate model outcomes are a predictable consequence of noise-mediated representational learning, rather than mere data imbalance.

ideas.title: Adapting First-Passage Time Methods from Chemical Reaction Kinetics to Neural Escape Problems
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established Kramers escape rate formalism from chemical reaction kinetics—where the Fokker-Planck equation is solved to find mean first-passage times over potential barriers—to the problem of deriving analytical escape times in neural systems. The analogous solution from chemistry treats escape as a diffusive process in a potential landscape, with the escape rate depending exponentially on barrier height and inversely on noise intensity. By mapping graded versus binary neural activation distributions onto different effective potential landscapes (e.g., smoother versus steeper wells), one can derive and compare closed-form escape time expressions. This adaptation provides a rigorous, physics-inspired framework to quantify how distribution granularity influences transition dynamics and stability in neural networks or decision circuits.

ideas.title: Apply Ecological Stability Analysis to Suicide Risk Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology, where network analysis is used to model complex systems like food webs and assess their stability. The analogous solution involves applying stability metrics—such as robustness, resilience, and keystone species identification—to predict ecosystem responses to disturbance. Adapted to suicide research, we model behavioral and physiological risk factors as an interactive network. We then apply ecological stability analysis to this network to quantify its robustness against the removal of factors, its resilience following a stressor (e.g., a traumatic event), and to identify 'keystone' risk factors that are central to the network's structure and dynamics. This approach moves beyond mapping correlations to predicting how the system of risk might respond to clinical or preventive interventions, prioritizing targets whose modulation could maximize systemic risk reduction.

ideas.title: Predictive disentanglement and validation of multi-source noise signatures in cortical population dynamics
ideas.description: This project moves beyond asking which noise source is 'dominant' to develop and test a predictive framework for how distinct biophysical noise sources (synaptic bombardment, stochastic ion channels, vesicle release, astrocytic modulation) combine to shape population-level neural statistics. We will use detailed network simulations to generate unique, quantitative signatures for each noise type and their interactions, focusing on measurable features like cross-neuron correlation structures and spike-train spectra. These theoretically derived signatures will then be used to re-analyze existing in vivo population recordings and design new experiments to validate or falsify the predicted contributions, ultimately building a causal model of noise composition in cortical computation.

ideas.title: Dynamic Causal Network Modeling of Suicidal Crisis Trajectories
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond static correlation maps by constructing personalized, dynamic causal networks of suicide risk factors. It uses intensive longitudinal monitoring of behavioral, cognitive, and physiological states in high-risk individuals to apply time-series network analysis and causal discovery methods. The goal is to identify individualized causal pathways and critical dynamic signatures (e.g., loss of network resilience, symptom cascades) that signal an impending crisis. This approach aims to provide a mechanistic, time-sensitive model for predicting acute risk and identifying personalized intervention targets.

ideas.title: Generalizable Control Policies for Stabilizing Behavioral Phenotypes Across Diverse Populations
ideas.description: This research fuses the need for external validation of behavioral classifiers with control theory's principles for stabilizing dynamic systems. The core aim is to develop control policies (e.g., via feedback loops in a digital intervention) designed to stabilize a spontaneous behavioral or cognitive state (like attention or mood), and then rigorously test these policies' efficacy and required parameter adjustments across independent, demographically and clinically diverse cohorts. This approach assesses not just if a model's predictions generalize, but if a derived stabilizing intervention remains effective and safe despite population-specific variations in baseline behavior and system noise. The synergy yields a methodology for creating population-robust dynamic interventions, where validation directly informs the adaptability of the control strategy itself.

ideas.title: Causal phenome-wide network discovery from latent factor models
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive factor analysis to infer the causal structure underlying the phenome. It first applies advanced factor or autoencoder models to the 2,291 traits to derive a set of robust, interpretable latent constructs, explicitly accounting for collinearity. It then employs causal discovery algorithms, leveraging genetic variants as natural sources of perturbation, to learn a directed network of causal influences among these latent factors. The final output is a causal phenome network that distinguishes mere correlation from likely causal pathways, providing a powerful framework for hypothesis generation and mechanistic understanding of disease and trait relationships.

ideas.title: Compare graded and binary representations using information-theoretic divergence instead of matched sparseness
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that graded and binary neural representations must be compared under matched sparseness levels. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where biodiversity comparisons between ecosystems of different sizes are solved using divergence metrics on normalized species distributions, the solution is adapted. The research idea is to compare graded and binary representations by treating their activity patterns as probability distributions and using information-theoretic divergences (e.g., KL, Jensen-Shannon) to measure their difference. This bypasses the need to enforce an artificial, fixed sparseness constraint, allowing each coding scheme to be evaluated under its natural distribution parameters for a fairer assessment of representational fidelity and efficiency.

ideas.title: Causal Triangulation for Genetically-Informed Resilience Factors
ideas.description: This system rigorously identifies behavioral or trait factors where evidence supports a causal, protective effect against substance use disorder (SUD) liability. It moves beyond simple genetic correlation by employing a sequential causal inference pipeline that controls for genetic confounding and tests robustness across methods. The core design integrates genomic SEM for latent modeling, Mendelian Randomization for causal testing, and within-family genetic designs for validation. This triangulation ensures identified resilience factors are robust candidates for mechanistic study and intervention development, minimizing false discoveries from population structure or pleiotropy.

ideas.title: Quantitatively defined minimal noise-sensitive attractor circuit discovery
ideas.description: This research first establishes a rigorous, multi-dimensional metric framework to define when graded distribution noise becomes 'significant' in attractor circuits. Using computational modeling of biological neural networks, it quantifies thresholds for functional reliability, dynamical stability, and information persistence degradation under stochastic inputs. The system then performs systematic topological minimization across known attractor circuit motifs, identifying the simplest architecture where any predefined noise significance boundary is crossed under realistic noise conditions. This produces a reproducible, quantitatively grounded 'minimal noise-sensitive circuit' that serves as a fundamental building block for understanding noise robustness in neural computation.

ideas.title: Adapting Population Genetics' Coalescent Theory to Analyze Noise-Induced State Transitions in Attractor Networks
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using stochastic field theory to study noise in large attractor networks. By analogy, this expansion draws from population genetics, specifically coalescent theory, which provides an analytical stochastic framework for tracing the ancestry of gene lineages in a population subject to drift and mutation. The analogous solution models how sampled lineages randomly coalesce backward in time to common ancestors. We adapt this solution to the network context by conceptualizing the noisy dynamical trajectories of neurons or units within an attractor state as lineages. The mathematical structure of the coalescent process is reformulated to run backward in time from a network's state near an attractor, modeling how noise causes these trajectories to merge into a common dynamical history or to diverge into a different attractor basin. This adapted framework aims to analytically derive the statistics of noise-induced transitions, such as the distribution of transition pathways and the scaling of mean first-passage times with network size and noise intensity, for very large attractor networks.

ideas.title: Modeling Suicide Risk Trajectories Using State-Space Methods from Ecology
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting state-space models (SSMs) from population ecology to investigate the temporal dynamics of suicide risk factors. In ecology, SSMs are used to infer latent population trajectories from imperfect longitudinal data, identifying how the sequence and timing of environmental stressors lead to population collapse. Adapted to suicide research, the method would model an individual's latent risk state over time, using longitudinal clinical or behavioral data. This allows researchers to separate observation error from true risk progression, identify critical sequences and time windows where risk factors interact (e.g., whether substance use exacerbates risk more if it follows vs. precedes a social loss), and potentially predict when the risk state crosses a critical threshold toward a suicide attempt.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Individual-Level Causal Interaction Model to Personalize Suicide Prevention Targeting
ideas.description: This research develops and validates a computational system that models the non-linear interactions and temporal dynamics among the 58 MR-supported causal factors for suicide attempts at the individual level. Instead of applying generic interventions, the system uses an individual's profile to simulate how modifying specific factors might cascade through their personal network of risks and protections, identifying the highest-leverage, most modifiable targets. This personalized causal shortlist is then used to tailor prevention strategies, which are tested for efficacy using adaptive, N-of-1 inspired trial designs embedded within clinical and community settings.

ideas.title: Adaptive Precision Switching in Neural Networks via Metabolic Cost-Benefit Control
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from cellular bioenergetics to the challenge of assuming networks actively regulate representation gradation. In cellular metabolism, organisms dynamically switch between high-precision, energy-costly processes and low-precision, energy-cheap ones based on metabolic sensors (e.g., AMPK) that monitor ATP/AMP ratios. Analogously, we propose modeling neural networks that incorporate a 'computational energy' metric and a meta-controller. This controller dynamically switches neuronal representations between graded (high precision, high cost) and binary (low precision, low cost) modes. The switching is governed by a continuous, learned assessment of task demand versus available computational resources, optimizing overall network performance for a given efficiency constraint, much like a cell optimizes its function for a given energy budget.
